旧{{jalink|県社}} |創建 = （伝）第12代{{jalink|景行天皇|Q329723}}53年 |本殿 = {{jalink|流造|Q210684}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|9月20日|Q2892}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=草薙神社|coordinates= {{coord2|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E}}}} }} {{座標一覧}} '''草薙神社'''（くさなぎじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|静岡県|Q131320}}{{jalink|静岡市|Q174691}}{{jalink|清水区|Q79385}}{{jalink|草薙 (静岡市)|Q11618222|草薙}}にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|式内社}}で、{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}は{{jalink|県社}}。 === 祭神 === 祭神は次の1柱神社由緒書。。 * '''{{jalink|ヤマトタケル|Q461258|日本武尊}}''' （やまとたけるのみこと） 『{{jalink|古事記|Q813031}}』『{{jalink|日本書紀|Q813935}}』の伝承によれば、ヤマトタケルの東征の際に賊がヤマトタケルのいる野に火をかけて焼き殺そうとしたが、ヤマトタケルは{{jalink|天叢雲剣|Q14944}}で草を薙ぎ払い向い火を放って難を逃れた。そのため天叢雲剣を「草薙剣」と称するという{{Sfn|草薙神社（平）|2000年}}。当社の北東方には同じく「クサナギ」を称する{{jalink|式内社}}として{{jalink|久佐奈岐神社|Q11368944}}（静岡市清水区山切）が知られるが、両社はともにそのヤマトタケルの火難伝説に関連する神社と伝えられ、一説に火難伝承地は当地に比定される{{Sfn|草薙神社（神々）|1987年}}ヤマトタケルの火難伝承地については当地（駿河国草薙）のほか、相模国小野や駿河国焼津に比定する説がある {{Harv|草薙神社（神々）|1987年}}。。 === 歴史 === === 創建 === [[File:Hyotanzuka-kofun (Shizuoka).JPG|thumb|200px|right|瓢箪塚古墳（清水区谷田）
{{Smaller|有度山北麓に分布する西ノ原古墳群の主墳。草薙神社付近に所在。}}]] 社伝では、{{jalink|景行天皇|Q329723}}53年に天皇が{{jalink|ヤマトタケル|Q461258|日本武尊}}ゆかりの地を巡幸した際、9月20日に天皇が当地に着き日本武尊の霊を奉斎したのが創建という。また、天皇は当社に{{jalink|神体|Q327532}}として{{jalink|草薙剣}}を奉納したが、この草薙剣が{{jalink|朱鳥|Q777413}}元年（{{jalink|686年|Q30619}}）に{{jalink|天武天皇|Q335181}}の勅命により{{jalink|熱田神宮|Q482065}}に移されたとも伝えている。また社伝では、当社は古くは清水区草薙1丁目の「天皇原（てんのうばら）」と称される地に鎮座したという。現在地への遷座時期は明らかでないが、一説に{{jalink|天正|Q1145058}}18年（{{jalink|1590年|Q6764}}）の造営時とする。 歴史考証の上では、前述の通り当社の創建にはヤマトタケル伝説が大きく関係するとされる。{{jalink|静岡市|Q174691}}一帯には、大規模古墳として{{jalink|葵区|Q1131483}}の{{jalink|谷津山古墳|Q18460128}}（全長110メートル）や{{jalink|清水区|Q79385}}の{{jalink|三池平古墳|Q107504282}}（全長65メートル）が残るが、これらの古墳を築いた勢力の服属が伝説成立の背景にあると推測される{{Sfn|草薙神社（神々）|1987年}}。なお、当地の首長古墳は「清水区庵原や谷津山丘陵（前期：4世紀代）→瀬名丘陵（中期：5世紀代）→有度山北麓（後期：6世紀代）」と変遷したと推測されるが{{Sfn|草薙神社（神々）|1987年}}{{Sfn|西ノ原古墳群（平）|1979年}}、これらのうち清水区庵原付近に{{jalink|久佐奈岐神社|Q11368944}}が、有度山北麓に当社が鎮座する。 また、「クサナギ」の名を焼畑系地名に由来するとする説もある{{Sfn|草薙神社（神々）|1987年}}。その中で、伝説の中でヤマトタケルが向火をつける様も野焼きの延焼防止としてよく知られる手法であることも併せて指摘され、先行する焼畑系地名に基いてヤマトタケルの火難伝説が成立したと推測される{{Sfn|草薙神社（神々）|1987年}}。ヤマトタケル伝説の成立以前の当社については、山の神として焼畑作物を与える神であったとも、谷部にあることから水の神であったとも考えられている{{Sfn|草薙神社（神々）|1987年}}。 === 概史 === {{jalink|延長 (元号)|Q1327274|延長}}5年（{{jalink|927年|Q30470}}）成立の『{{jalink|延喜式|Q1342448}}』{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|神名帳}}では{{jalink|駿河国|Q864170}}{{jalink|有度郡}}に「草薙神社」と記載され、{{jalink|式内社}}に列している{{Sfn|草薙神社（平）|2000年}}。ヤマトタケル伝承との関連から武家から信仰され、{{jalink|天慶|Q1327340}}年間（{{jalink|877年|Q23905}}-{{jalink|885年|Q24894}}）には{{jalink|承平天慶の乱#平将門の乱|Q2975322|平将門の乱}}の平定に奉賽がなされたと伝える{{Sfn|草薙神社（平）|2000年}}。 {{jalink|中世|Q12554}}には、{{jalink|永享|Q1193059}}4年（{{jalink|1432年|Q6349}}）に将軍{{jalink|足利義教|Q239170}}の駿河下向に従った{{jalink|尭孝|Q11464813}}が草薙社について記述している{{Sfn|草薙神社（平）|2000年}}。{{jalink|天正|Q1145058}}8年（{{jalink|1580年|Q6712}}）には、願主森民部太夫により釣燈籠と鰐口（ともに静岡市指定文化財）が奉納された{{Sfn|草薙神社（平）|2000年}}。天正10年（{{jalink|1582年|Q6719}}）には竹木の伐採が禁止され、天正18年（{{jalink|1590年|Q6764}}）には社領として草薙郷18石が社人の民部大輔に安堵された{{Sfn|草薙神社（平）|2000年}}。この社領は、{{jalink|慶長|Q1328185}}4年（{{jalink|1599年|Q6803}}）にも横田村詮により安堵されている{{Sfn|草薙神社（平）|2000年}}。 江戸時代の社領（{{jalink|朱印地}}）は50石{{Sfn|草薙神社（平）|2000年}}。 {{jalink|明治維新|Q8707}}後、{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}3年（{{jalink|1873年|Q7757}}）に{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534}}において{{jalink|郷社|Q134917316}}に列し、明治12年（{{jalink|1879年|Q7783}}）7月24日に{{jalink|県社}}に昇格した{{Sfn|明治神社誌料|1912年}}。 === 神階 === * 正五位下 （『駿河国神名帳』） - 表記は「草奈岐天神」。 === 境内 === 境内は有度山北麓、草薙川上流の谷部に位置する{{Sfn|草薙神社（神々）|1987年}}。広さは0.7ヘクタール。 境内に立つクスノキは幹心が枯れ、外皮のみを残すが、樹高6メートル・周囲25メートルで樹齢は1,000年以上と言われる古木で、静岡市指定天然記念物に指定されている。 {{jalink|草薙駅 (JR東海)|Q871079|草薙駅}}付近の平地は「天皇原（てんのうばら）」と称される。かつて草薙神社はこの天皇原の地にあったといわれ、その地は現在も「古宮」として伝わっている（{{Coord|35|0|4.61|N|138|26|39.00|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|name=古宮（旧社地）}}）現地説明板。。 zuishinmon.JPG|随身門 File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) kusu.JPG|大樟（静岡市指定天然記念物） File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) torii.JPG|鳥居 File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) furumiya.JPG|古宮 === 大鳥居 === [[File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) ichinotorii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|一の鳥居]] 草薙駅そばの市道をまたいで建つコンクリート製「一の鳥居」が、1950年代から存在していたが、2020年に撤去された。 ==== 撤去 ==== 2019年に一部表面に破損が発生していることが確認された。2018年頃より地域住民や氏子より草薙神社に管理の問い合わせがあったため神社側で改めて確認したところ、神社の所有財産ではなかったことがわかり、市や県の過去の記録が確認される事態となった。その結果、1975年に静岡県が事業主体となる区画整理の一環で市道の拡幅工事を行った際に、原状回復補償として県が同じ場所に新たな道幅に合わせて再建したものの、その時点で所有者不明だったことが改めて判明した。つまり、1970年代よりずっと、修繕等の管理が一切ないまま地元のシンボルとして存在していたことになる{{Cite news|url=https://www.at-s.com/news/article/topics/shizuoka/723682.html |title=鳥居老朽化、持ち主は？　静岡・草薙神社参道入り口|date=2020-01-08|publisher=アットエス静岡新聞SBS}}。 老朽化による倒壊の危険性があるため周囲住民より撤去の要望が出され、静岡市は2020年3月24日より同年6月15日にかけて、所有者に対して除却を命ずる公告を行ったが{{PDFlink|[https://www.city.shizuoka.lg.jp/000848750.pdf 南幹線の大鳥居及び石碑２基の所有者に対して除却を命ずる公告をします！]}}（静岡市報道資料、2020年3月24日）、所有者が名乗り出ることはなかったため、市が鳥居脇の石碑2つの撤去と周辺の電話や電気等のケーブル移設費用を含め1100万円の費用を出して強制的な略式代執行を行う方針を発表し{{PDFlink|[https://www.city.shizuoka.lg.jp/000864003.pdf 南幹線の鳥居等を代執行により除却します]}}（静岡市報道資料、2020年8月17日）、同年内に撤去された。 毎日新聞記者による調査・取材によると同紙東京版2020年10月31日夕刊社会面（9頁）、地元の有志多数による出資で、直接には神社が関与せず、建てられたことを傍証する地方紙（{{jalink|静岡新聞|Q904670}}）の記事などは見つかっており、そういった経緯から所有権や所有者について不明となったのだろうという。似たような例で著名なものとして、芝{{jalink|増上寺#旧・総門「大門」|Q249139|増上寺の大門}}が何らかの理由で都の財産目録から抜け落ち、所有者を確認することができなくなっていた例など、宗教関係の建物では「みんなのもの」という意識が元で、所有者・管理者がはっきりしなくなっている場合が多く、この場合では1975年の再建時に不明のままとしたことが撤去につながった、というコメントがある。 === 摂末社 === 境内社として本殿南側に9社、北側に9社が鎮座する。 File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) kita-massha.JPG|北側末社 File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) minami-massha.JPG|南側末社 === 祭事 === {{jalink|例祭|Q11385469}}は9月20日で、この日は景行天皇による神社創建日であると伝える。当日には「{{jalink|龍勢|Q11679206}}（流星）煙火」と称される、{{jalink|狼煙|Q659892}}から発展した{{jalink|花火|Q127933}}が打ち上げられる。この煙火は{{jalink|安政|Q1327259}}年間（{{jalink|1854年|Q7670}}-{{jalink|1860年|Q7691}}）から続くといい、静岡県指定無形民俗文化財に指定されている。 === 文化財 === === 静岡県指定無形民俗文化財 === * 草薙神社龍勢花火 - 平成15年3月24日指定。 === 静岡市指定文化財 === * 有形文化財 ** 釣燈籠（工芸品） - {{jalink|室町時代|Q334845}}末期、{{jalink|天正|Q1145058}}8年（{{jalink|1580年|Q6712}}）の森民部太夫による奉納。昭和37年9月17日指定。 ** 鰐口（工芸品） - 室町時代末期、天正8年（1580年）の森民部太夫による奉納。昭和37年9月17日指定。 * 天然記念物 ** 大樟 - 昭和37年9月17日指定。 === 現地情報 === '''所在地''' * {{jalink|静岡県|Q131320}}{{jalink|静岡市|Q174691}}{{jalink|清水区|Q79385}}{{jalink|草薙 (静岡市)|Q11618222|草薙}}349 '''交通アクセス''' * {{jalink|東海旅客鉄道|Q513679}}（JR東海）{{jalink|東海道線 (静岡地区)|Q11527981|東海道本線}} {{jalink|草薙駅 (JR東海)|Q871079|草薙駅}}から ** バス：しずてつジャストライン（三保草薙線）で「草薙神社前」バス停下車 （乗車時間約7分、下車後徒歩すぐ） '''周辺''' * 瓢箪塚古墳（西ノ原1号墳、{{ウィキ座標|34|59|53.74|N|138|26|40.83|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|位置|name=瓢箪塚古墳}}） - 全長約45.8メートルの前方後円墳{{Sfn|西ノ原古墳群（平）|1979年}}。 === 脚注 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''注釈''' {{reflist|group="注"}} 拝殿 |所在地 = {{jalink|静岡県|Q131320}}{{jalink|静岡市|Q174691}}{{jalink|清水区|Q79385}}{{jalink|草薙 (静岡市)|Q11618222|草薙}}349 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=草薙神社}} |祭神 = {{jalink|ヤマトタケル|Q461258|日本武尊}} |神体 = |社格 = {{jalink|式内社}}（小）
旧{{jalink|県社}} |創建 = （伝）第12代{{jalink|景行天皇|Q329723}}53年 |本殿 = {{jalink|流造|Q210684}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|9月20日|Q2892}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=草薙神社|coordinates= {{coord2|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E}}}} }} {{座標一覧}} '''草薙神社'''（くさなぎじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|静岡県|Q131320}}{{jalink|静岡市|Q174691}}{{jalink|清水区|Q79385}}{{jalink|草薙 (静岡市)|Q11618222|草薙}}にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|式内社}}で、{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}は{{jalink|県社}}。 === 祭神 === 祭神は次の1柱神社由緒書。。 * '''{{jalink|ヤマトタケル|Q461258|日本武尊}}''' （やまとたけるのみこと） 『{{jalink|古事記|Q813031}}』『{{jalink|日本書紀|Q813935}}』の伝承によれば、ヤマトタケルの東征の際に賊がヤマトタケルのいる野に火をかけて焼き殺そうとしたが、ヤマトタケルは{{jalink|天叢雲剣|Q14944}}で草を薙ぎ払い向い火を放って難を逃れた。そのため天叢雲剣を「草薙剣」と称するという{{Sfn|草薙神社（平）|2000年}}。当社の北東方には同じく「クサナギ」を称する{{jalink|式内社}}として{{jalink|久佐奈岐神社|Q11368944}}（静岡市清水区山切）が知られるが、両社はともにそのヤマトタケルの火難伝説に関連する神社と伝えられ、一説に火難伝承地は当地に比定される{{Sfn|草薙神社（神々）|1987年}}ヤマトタケルの火難伝承地については当地（駿河国草薙）のほか、相模国小野や駿河国焼津に比定する説がある {{Harv|草薙神社（神々）|1987年}}。。 === 歴史 === === 創建 === [[File:Hyotanzuka-kofun (Shizuoka).JPG|thumb|200px|right|瓢箪塚古墳（清水区谷田）
{{Smaller|有度山北麓に分布する西ノ原古墳群の主墳。草薙神社付近に所在。}}]] 社伝では、{{jalink|景行天皇|Q329723}}53年に天皇が{{jalink|ヤマトタケル|Q461258|日本武尊}}ゆかりの地を巡幸した際、9月20日に天皇が当地に着き日本武尊の霊を奉斎したのが創建という。また、天皇は当社に{{jalink|神体|Q327532}}として{{jalink|草薙剣}}を奉納したが、この草薙剣が{{jalink|朱鳥|Q777413}}元年（{{jalink|686年|Q30619}}）に{{jalink|天武天皇|Q335181}}の勅命により{{jalink|熱田神宮|Q482065}}に移されたとも伝えている。また社伝では、当社は古くは清水区草薙1丁目の「天皇原（てんのうばら）」と称される地に鎮座したという。現在地への遷座時期は明らかでないが、一説に{{jalink|天正|Q1145058}}18年（{{jalink|1590年|Q6764}}）の造営時とする。 歴史考証の上では、前述の通り当社の創建にはヤマトタケル伝説が大きく関係するとされる。{{jalink|静岡市|Q174691}}一帯には、大規模古墳として{{jalink|葵区|Q1131483}}の{{jalink|谷津山古墳|Q18460128}}（全長110メートル）や{{jalink|清水区|Q79385}}の{{jalink|三池平古墳|Q107504282}}（全長65メートル）が残るが、これらの古墳を築いた勢力の服属が伝説成立の背景にあると推測される{{Sfn|草薙神社（神々）|1987年}}。なお、当地の首長古墳は「清水区庵原や谷津山丘陵（前期：4世紀代）→瀬名丘陵（中期：5世紀代）→有度山北麓（後期：6世紀代）」と変遷したと推測されるが{{Sfn|草薙神社（神々）|1987年}}{{Sfn|西ノ原古墳群（平）|1979年}}、これらのうち清水区庵原付近に{{jalink|久佐奈岐神社|Q11368944}}が、有度山北麓に当社が鎮座する。 また、「クサナギ」の名を焼畑系地名に由来するとする説もある{{Sfn|草薙神社（神々）|1987年}}。その中で、伝説の中でヤマトタケルが向火をつける様も野焼きの延焼防止としてよく知られる手法であることも併せて指摘され、先行する焼畑系地名に基いてヤマトタケルの火難伝説が成立したと推測される{{Sfn|草薙神社（神々）|1987年}}。ヤマトタケル伝説の成立以前の当社については、山の神として焼畑作物を与える神であったとも、谷部にあることから水の神であったとも考えられている{{Sfn|草薙神社（神々）|1987年}}。 === 概史 === {{jalink|延長 (元号)|Q1327274|延長}}5年（{{jalink|927年|Q30470}}）成立の『{{jalink|延喜式|Q1342448}}』{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|神名帳}}では{{jalink|駿河国|Q864170}}{{jalink|有度郡}}に「草薙神社」と記載され、{{jalink|式内社}}に列している{{Sfn|草薙神社（平）|2000年}}。ヤマトタケル伝承との関連から武家から信仰され、{{jalink|天慶|Q1327340}}年間（{{jalink|877年|Q23905}}-{{jalink|885年|Q24894}}）には{{jalink|承平天慶の乱#平将門の乱|Q2975322|平将門の乱}}の平定に奉賽がなされたと伝える{{Sfn|草薙神社（平）|2000年}}。 {{jalink|中世|Q12554}}には、{{jalink|永享|Q1193059}}4年（{{jalink|1432年|Q6349}}）に将軍{{jalink|足利義教|Q239170}}の駿河下向に従った{{jalink|尭孝|Q11464813}}が草薙社について記述している{{Sfn|草薙神社（平）|2000年}}。{{jalink|天正|Q1145058}}8年（{{jalink|1580年|Q6712}}）には、願主森民部太夫により釣燈籠と鰐口（ともに静岡市指定文化財）が奉納された{{Sfn|草薙神社（平）|2000年}}。天正10年（{{jalink|1582年|Q6719}}）には竹木の伐採が禁止され、天正18年（{{jalink|1590年|Q6764}}）には社領として草薙郷18石が社人の民部大輔に安堵された{{Sfn|草薙神社（平）|2000年}}。この社領は、{{jalink|慶長|Q1328185}}4年（{{jalink|1599年|Q6803}}）にも横田村詮により安堵されている{{Sfn|草薙神社（平）|2000年}}。 江戸時代の社領（{{jalink|朱印地}}）は50石{{Sfn|草薙神社（平）|2000年}}。 {{jalink|明治維新|Q8707}}後、{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}3年（{{jalink|1873年|Q7757}}）に{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534}}において{{jalink|郷社|Q134917316}}に列し、明治12年（{{jalink|1879年|Q7783}}）7月24日に{{jalink|県社}}に昇格した{{Sfn|明治神社誌料|1912年}}。 === 神階 === * 正五位下 （『駿河国神名帳』） - 表記は「草奈岐天神」。 === 境内 === 境内は有度山北麓、草薙川上流の谷部に位置する{{Sfn|草薙神社（神々）|1987年}}。広さは0.7ヘクタール。 境内に立つクスノキは幹心が枯れ、外皮のみを残すが、樹高6メートル・周囲25メートルで樹齢は1,000年以上と言われる古木で、静岡市指定天然記念物に指定されている。 {{jalink|草薙駅 (JR東海)|Q871079|草薙駅}}付近の平地は「天皇原（てんのうばら）」と称される。かつて草薙神社はこの天皇原の地にあったといわれ、その地は現在も「古宮」として伝わっている（{{Coord|35|0|4.61|N|138|26|39.00|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|name=古宮（旧社地）}}）現地説明板。。 zuishinmon.JPG|随身門 File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) kusu.JPG|大樟（静岡市指定天然記念物） File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) torii.JPG|鳥居 File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) furumiya.JPG|古宮 === 大鳥居 === [[File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) ichinotorii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|一の鳥居]] 草薙駅そばの市道をまたいで建つコンクリート製「一の鳥居」が、1950年代から存在していたが、2020年に撤去された。 ==== 撤去 ==== 2019年に一部表面に破損が発生していることが確認された。2018年頃より地域住民や氏子より草薙神社に管理の問い合わせがあったため神社側で改めて確認したところ、神社の所有財産ではなかったことがわかり、市や県の過去の記録が確認される事態となった。その結果、1975年に静岡県が事業主体となる区画整理の一環で市道の拡幅工事を行った際に、原状回復補償として県が同じ場所に新たな道幅に合わせて再建したものの、その時点で所有者不明だったことが改めて判明した。つまり、1970年代よりずっと、修繕等の管理が一切ないまま地元のシンボルとして存在していたことになる{{Cite news|url=https://www.at-s.com/news/article/topics/shizuoka/723682.html |title=鳥居老朽化、持ち主は？　静岡・草薙神社参道入り口|date=2020-01-08|publisher=アットエス静岡新聞SBS}}。 老朽化による倒壊の危険性があるため周囲住民より撤去の要望が出され、静岡市は2020年3月24日より同年6月15日にかけて、所有者に対して除却を命ずる公告を行ったが{{PDFlink|[https://www.city.shizuoka.lg.jp/000848750.pdf 南幹線の大鳥居及び石碑２基の所有者に対して除却を命ずる公告をします！]}}（静岡市報道資料、2020年3月24日）、所有者が名乗り出ることはなかったため、市が鳥居脇の石碑2つの撤去と周辺の電話や電気等のケーブル移設費用を含め1100万円の費用を出して強制的な略式代執行を行う方針を発表し{{PDFlink|[https://www.city.shizuoka.lg.jp/000864003.pdf 南幹線の鳥居等を代執行により除却します]}}（静岡市報道資料、2020年8月17日）、同年内に撤去された。 毎日新聞記者による調査・取材によると同紙東京版2020年10月31日夕刊社会面（9頁）、地元の有志多数による出資で、直接には神社が関与せず、建てられたことを傍証する地方紙（{{jalink|静岡新聞|Q904670}}）の記事などは見つかっており、そういった経緯から所有権や所有者について不明となったのだろうという。似たような例で著名なものとして、芝{{jalink|増上寺#旧・総門「大門」|Q249139|増上寺の大門}}が何らかの理由で都の財産目録から抜け落ち、所有者を確認することができなくなっていた例など、宗教関係の建物では「みんなのもの」という意識が元で、所有者・管理者がはっきりしなくなっている場合が多く、この場合では1975年の再建時に不明のままとしたことが撤去につながった、というコメントがある。 === 摂末社 === 境内社として本殿南側に9社、北側に9社が鎮座する。 File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) kita-massha.JPG|北側末社 File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) minami-massha.JPG|南側末社 === 祭事 === {{jalink|例祭|Q11385469}}は9月20日で、この日は景行天皇による神社創建日であると伝える。当日には「{{jalink|龍勢|Q11679206}}（流星）煙火」と称される、{{jalink|狼煙|Q659892}}から発展した{{jalink|花火|Q127933}}が打ち上げられる。この煙火は{{jalink|安政|Q1327259}}年間（{{jalink|1854年|Q7670}}-{{jalink|1860年|Q7691}}）から続くといい、静岡県指定無形民俗文化財に指定されている。 === 文化財 === === 静岡県指定無形民俗文化財 === * 草薙神社龍勢花火 - 平成15年3月24日指定。 === 静岡市指定文化財 === * 有形文化財 ** 釣燈籠（工芸品） - {{jalink|室町時代|Q334845}}末期、{{jalink|天正|Q1145058}}8年（{{jalink|1580年|Q6712}}）の森民部太夫による奉納。昭和37年9月17日指定。 ** 鰐口（工芸品） - 室町時代末期、天正8年（1580年）の森民部太夫による奉納。昭和37年9月17日指定。 * 天然記念物 ** 大樟 - 昭和37年9月17日指定。 === 現地情報 === '''所在地''' * {{jalink|静岡県|Q131320}}{{jalink|静岡市|Q174691}}{{jalink|清水区|Q79385}}{{jalink|草薙 (静岡市)|Q11618222|草薙}}349 '''交通アクセス''' * {{jalink|東海旅客鉄道|Q513679}}（JR東海）{{jalink|東海道線 (静岡地区)|Q11527981|東海道本線}} {{jalink|草薙駅 (JR東海)|Q871079|草薙駅}}から ** バス：しずてつジャストライン（三保草薙線）で「草薙神社前」バス停下車 （乗車時間約7分、下車後徒歩すぐ） '''周辺''' * 瓢箪塚古墳（西ノ原1号墳、{{ウィキ座標|34|59|53.74|N|138|26|40.83|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|位置|name=瓢箪塚古墳}}） - 全長約45.8メートルの前方後円墳{{Sfn|西ノ原古墳群（平）|1979年}}。 === 脚注 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''注釈''' {{reflist|group="注"}} Old {{jalink|Prefectural company}} |Foundation = (tradition) 12th {{jalink|Emperor Keiko|Q329723}}53 |Main shrine = {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{jalink|September 20th|Q2892}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Kusanagi Shrine|coordinates={{coord2|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E}}}} }} {{List of coordinates}} '''Kusanagi Shrine'' (Kusanagi Shrine) is located in {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} located in (Shizuoka City)|Q11618222|Kusanagi}}. {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534|Old company rank}} is {{jalink|Prefectural company}}. === Enshrined God === The enshrined deity is one of the followingShrine Historysho. . * '''{{jalink|Yamato Takeru|Q461258|Nippon Takeru}}''' (Yamato Takeru Mikoto) According to the legends of "{{jalink|Kojiki|Q813031}}" and "{{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}," during Yamato Takeru's eastern expedition, bandits They tried to set the field where Totakeru was on fire and burn it to death, but Yamatotakeru escaped by mowing down the grass with {{jalink|Amamuragumo Sword|Q14944}} and setting fire to it. For this reason, the Ten Murakumo sword is called the ``Kusanagi sword'' {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. To the northeast of our company is {{jalink|Kusanagi Shrine|Q11368944}} (Yamakiri, Shimizu Ward, Shizuoka City), which is also known as {{jalink|Shikiuchi}} and also called "Kusanagi." However, both shrines are said to be related to the legend of Yamato Takeru's fire disaster, and one theory suggests that this is where the legend of the fire disaster occurred.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}In addition to this place (Kusanagi, Suruga Province), there is a theory that the place where Yamato Takeru's fire disaster is said to have been said is compared to Ono, Sagami Province, and Yaizu, Suruga Province {{Harv|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. . === History === === Founding === [[File:Hyotanzuka-kofun (Shizuoka).JPG|thumb|200px|right|Hyotanzuka Tumulus (Yada, Shimizu Ward)
{{Smaller|The main tomb of the Nishinohara kofun group located at the northern foot of Mt. Udo. Located near Kusanagi Shrine. }}]] According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in {{jalink|Emperor Keiko|Q329723}} in 1953 when the Emperor visited places related to {{jalink|Yamato Takeru|Q461258|Nihon Takeru}}, and the Emperor arrived on September 20th to enshrine the spirit of Nihon Takeru. In addition, the Emperor dedicated the {{jalink|Kusanagi Sword}} to our company as {{jalink|Shintai|Q327532}}, and this Kusanagi Sword was discovered in the first year of {{jalink|Shudori|Q777413}} ({{ja It is also said that it was moved to {{jalink|Atsuta Shrine|Q482065}} by the imperial order of {{jalink|Emperor Tenmu|Q335181}} in 686|Q30619}}.. Also, according to the company's legend, the company was originally located in a place called ``Tennoubara'' in Kusanagi 1-chome, Shimizu Ward. It is not clear when it was moved to its current location, but one theory is that it was built in {{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}}18 ({{jalink|1590|Q6764}}).Based on historical research, as mentioned above, the founding of our company is said to have a great deal to do with the legend of Yamato Takeru. In the {{jalink|Shizuoka City|Q174691}} area, there are large-scale burial mounds such as {{jalink|Yatsuyama Tumulus|Q18460128}} (total length 110 meters) in {{jalink|Aoi Ward|Q1131483}} and {{jalink|Shimizu The {{jalink|Miikedaira Tumulus|Q107504282}} (total length 65 meters) in Ku|Q79385}} remains, and it is speculated that the subordination of the powers that built these tombs is behind the legend {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. It is assumed that the chief burial mounds in this area have changed over the course of ``Shimizu-ku Ibara and Yatsuyama Hills (early 4th century) → Sena Hills (middle 5th century) → North foot of Mt. Udo (late 6th century)'' {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods) |1987}}{{Sfn|Nishinohara Tumulus Group (Taira)|1979}} Among these, {{jalink|Kusanagi Shrine|Q11368944}} is located near Ibara in Shimizu-ku, and our company is located at the northern foot of Mt. Udo. There is also a theory that the name ``Kusanagi'' comes from a place name related to slash-and-burn cultivation {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. It has also been pointed out that the legend of Yamato Takeru lighting fires is a well-known method for preventing the spread of wildfires, and it is assumed that the legend of Yamato Takeru's fire disaster was established based on the preceding slash-and-burn place names {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. Before the legend of Yamato Takeru was established, it is believed that the shrine was the god of the mountains and the god of slash-and-burn crops, or the god of water since it was located in a valley {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. === Outline === {{jalink|extension (Era name)|Q1327274|Extended}} "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932| In the Shinmeicho}}, ``Kusanagi Shrine'' is written in {{jalink|Suruga Province|Q864170}}{{jalink|Arido District}}, and it is listed as {{jalink|Shikiuchi}}{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. It was worshiped by samurai families due to its connection with the Yamato Takeru tradition, and was worshiped for {{jalink|Tenkei|Q1327340}} years ({{jalink|877 years|Q23905}}-{{jalink|885 years| Q24894}}) says that an offering was made to pacify the {{jalink|Johei Tenkyo Rebellion#Taira no Masakado Rebellion|Q2975322|Taira no Masakado Rebellion}} {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}.In the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}}, the shogun {{jalink|Eikyo|Q1193059}}4 ({{jalink|1432|Q6349}}) {{jalink|Yoshitaka|Q11464813}}, who followed |Ashikaga Yoshinori|Q239170}} to Suruga Shimoki, wrote about Kusanagi Shrine {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. In {{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}}8 ({{jalink|1580|Q6712}}), a fishing lantern and a crocodile mouth (both cultural properties designated by Shizuoka City) were dedicated to the shrine by the petitioner, Moriminbu Dayu.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. In the 10th year of Tensho ({{jalink|1582|Q6719}}), felling of bamboo and trees was prohibited, and in the 18th year of Tensho ({{jalink|1590|Q6764}}), 18 koku of Kusanagi-go was granted as shrine territory by Daisuke Minbu, a social worker {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. This shrine territory was also relieved by Akira Yokotamura in {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}4 ({{jalink|1599|Q6803}}) {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. The shrine territory ({{jalink|shuinji}}) in the Edo period was 50 koku {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}.After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}3 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}), in {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534}} It was ranked as {{jalink|gosha|Q134917316}} and was promoted to {{jalink|prefectural shrine}} on July 24, 1879|1912}}. === God level === *Shogoi Lower Rank (``Suruga Province Shinto Name Book'') - Written as ``Kusanagi Tenjin.'' === Precincts === The shrine grounds are located in the valley of the upper reaches of the Kusanagi River at the northern foot of Mt. Udo.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. The area is 0.7 hectares. The trunk of the camphor tree that stands within the shrine grounds has withered and only the outer skin remains, but it is an old tree that is 6 meters high and 25 meters in circumference and is said to be over 1,000 years old, and has been designated as a natural monument by Shizuoka City. The flat land near {{jalink|Kusanagi Station (JR Tokai)|Q871079|Kusanagi Station}} is called "Tennoubara" . It is said that Kusanagi Shrine was once located on this land of Emperor Hara, and the place is still known as the "Old Palace." . File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) keidai.JPG|Precincts File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) zuishinmon.JPG|ZushinmonFile:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) kusu.JPG|Big Camphor (Shizuoka City designated natural monument) File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) torii.JPG|Torii File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) furumiya.JPG|Furumiya === Otorii === [[File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) ichinotorii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Ichino Torii]] The concrete "Ichi-no-Torii" gate, which was built across the city road near Kusanagi Station, had existed since the 1950s, but was removed in 2020. ==== Removal ==== In 2019, it was confirmed that some surface damage had occurred. Since around 2018, Kusanagi Shrine has received inquiries about its management from local residents and parishioners, and when the shrine checked again, it was discovered that the property was not owned by the shrine, and past records of the city and prefecture had to be checked. As a result, in 1975, when Shizuoka Prefecture carried out widening work on the city road as part of the land readjustment project, the prefecture rebuilt the road in the same location to match the new road width as compensation for restoring it to its original condition, but it was once again revealed that the owner was unknown at that time. In other words, it has existed as a local symbol since the 1970s without any maintenance or maintenance. Shizuoka/Kusanagi Shrine approach entrance|date=2020-01-08|publisher=AtS Shizuoka Shimbun SBS}}.Due to the risk of collapse due to deterioration, surrounding residents requested its removal, and Shizuoka City issued a public notice ordering the owner to remove it from March 24, 2020 to June 15 of the same year. We will issue a public notice ordering the owners of the large torii gate and two stone monuments on the South Main Line to remove them! ]}} (Shizuoka City Press Materials, March 24, 2020) Since the owner did not come forward, the city announced a plan to carry out a compulsory summary execution at a cost of 11 million yen, including the cost of removing the two stone monuments next to the torii gate and relocating surrounding telephone and electric cables.{{PDFlink|[https://www.city.shizuoka.lg.jp/000864003.pdf Torii gates, etc. on the South Main Line will be removed by proxy execution]}} (Shizuoka City press material, August 17, 2020), and were removed within the same year.According to research and interviews by Mainichi Shimbun reporters, The evening edition social section of the Tokyo edition of October 31, 2020 (p. 9), articles were found in local newspapers ({{jalink|Shizuoka Shimbun|Q904670}}) that corroborated that the building was built with funding from many local volunteers, with no direct involvement of the shrine, and that is probably why the ownership and ownership became unclear. A similar and well-known example is the case where the Shiba {{jalink|Zojoji Temple #Old Main Gate ``Daimon''|Q249139|Zojoji Daimon}} fell out of the capital's property inventory for some reason, making it impossible to confirm its owner. , there is a comment that in religious buildings, the ownership and management are often unclear due to the idea that they belong to everyone, and in this case, it was left unclear when the building was rebuilt in 1975, which led to its demolition. === Setsusha === There are 9 shrines on the south side of the main shrine and 9 shrines on the north side of the shrine. File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) kita-massha.JPG|Kitaside Suesha File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) minami-massha.JPG|South side shrine === Festival === The {{jalink|regular festival|Q11385469}} is held on September 20th, and this day is said to be the day the shrine was founded by Emperor Keiko. On that day, {{jalink|fireworks|Q127933}}, which evolved from {{jalink|fireworks|Q127933}}, called "{{jalink|dragon force|Q11679206}} (meteor) fireworks" will be launched. These fireworks are said to have continued for the {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}} years ({{jalink|1854|Q7670}}-{{jalink|1860|Q7691}}), and are designated as an intangible folk cultural property by Shizuoka Prefecture. === Cultural assets === === Shizuoka Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * Kusanagi Shrine Ryusei Fireworks - Designated on March 24, 2003. === Cultural property designated by Shizuoka City === *Tangible cultural property ** Fishing lantern (craft) - Dedicated by Morimbu Tayu in {{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}}8 ({{jalink|1580|Q6712}}) at the end of the {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}}. Designated on September 17, 1960. ** Waniguchi (craft) - Dedicated by Morimbu Tayu in 1580 at the end of the Muromachi period. Designated on September 17, 1960. * Natural monument ** Big Camphor - Designated on September 17, 1960. === Local information === '''Location''' * {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}{{jalink|Shizuoka City|Q174691}}{{jalink|Shimizu Ward|Q79385}}{{jalink|Kusanagi (Shizuoka City)|Q11618222|Kusanagi}}349 '''Access''' * From {{jalink|Tokai Passenger Railway|Q513679}} (JR Tokai) {{jalink|Tokaido Line (Shizuoka Area)|Q11527981|Tokaido Main Line}} {{jalink|Kusanagi Station (JR Tokai)|Q871079|Kusanagi Station}} ** Bus: Take the Shizutetsu Just Line (Miho Kusanagi Line) and get off at the "Kusanagi Jinja-mae" bus stop (about 7 minutes on the bus, a short walk after getting off) '''Surroundings''' * Hyōtanzuka Tumulus (Nishinohara No. 1 Tumulus, {{Wiki coordinates|34|59|53.74|N|138|26|40.83|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|location|name=Hyotanzuka Tumulus}}) - A keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of approximately 45.8 meters {{Sfn|Nishinohara Tumulus Group (Taira)|1979}}. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="Note"}} '''Source''' {{reflist|2}} === References === * Shrine history book * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Meiji Shrine Library|author=|year=1912|chapter=Kusanagi Shrine|title={{jalink|Meiji Shrine Library|Q11486103}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Library|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine Library|1912}}}} ** [{{NDLDC|1088244/883}} "Meiji Shrine Journal Materials Fuken Gosha Volume 1"] (National Diet Library Digital Collection) Reference frames 883-884. * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Kenichi Tanikawa|editor-link=Kenichi Tanikawa|author=Kanichi Nomoto|authorlink=Kanichi Nomoto|year=1987|chapter=Kusanagi Shrine|title=Japanese Gods -Shrines and Sacred Sites- 10 Tokai|publisher={{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}|isbn=4560022208|ref={{Harvid|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title=Japanese Historical Place Name System 22 Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}|isbn=4582490220|ref=}}** {{Wikicite|reference="Kusanagi Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Nishinohara Tumulus Group"|ref={{Harvid|Nishinohara Tumulus Group (Taira)|1979}}}} ===External link=== [https://kusanagijinjya.jp/ Kusanagi Shrine official website] === Related items === {{Commonscat|Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)}} * {{jalink|Amamuragumo Ken|Q14944}} (Kusanagi Ken) * {{jalink|Kusanagi Shrine|Q11368944}} * {{jalink|Yaizu Shrine|Q3571342}} * {{jalink|Katori Shrine|Q372380}} - {{wikidata link|Q11618229}} == Japanese content == {{Otheruses|Shrine in Kusanagi, Shimizu Ward, Shizuoka City|Shrine in Yamakiri, Shimizu Ward with the same reading|Kusanagi Shrine|Shrine in Ise City, Mie Prefecture|Kusanagi Shrine}} {{Shrine |Name = Kusanagi Shrine |Image = [[File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) haiden.JPG|280px]]
House of worship |Location = {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}{{jalink|Shizuoka City|Q174691}}{{jalink|Shimizu Ward|Q79385}}{{jalink|Kusanagi (Shizuoka City)|Q11618222|Kusanagi}}349 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 steps degrees minutes seconds|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kusanagi Shrine}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Yamato Takeru|Q461258|Japan Takeru}} |Divinity = |Company rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
Old {{jalink|Prefectural company}} |Foundation = (tradition) 12th {{jalink|Emperor Keiko|Q329723}}53 |Main shrine = {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{jalink|September 20th|Q2892}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Kusanagi Shrine|coordinates={{coord2|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E}}}} }} {{List of coordinates}} '''Kusanagi Shrine'' (Kusanagi Shrine) is located in {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} located in (Shizuoka City)|Q11618222|Kusanagi}}. {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534|Old company rank}} is {{jalink|Prefectural company}}. === Enshrined God === The enshrined deity is one of the followingShrine Historysho. . * '''{{jalink|Yamato Takeru|Q461258|Nippon Takeru}}''' (Yamato Takeru Mikoto) According to the legends of "{{jalink|Kojiki|Q813031}}" and "{{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}," during Yamato Takeru's eastern expedition, bandits They tried to set the field where Totakeru was on fire and burn it to death, but Yamatotakeru escaped by mowing down the grass with {{jalink|Amamuragumo Sword|Q14944}} and setting fire to it. For this reason, the Ten Murakumo sword is called the ``Kusanagi sword'' {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. To the northeast of our company is {{jalink|Kusanagi Shrine|Q11368944}} (Yamakiri, Shimizu Ward, Shizuoka City), which is also known as {{jalink|Shikiuchi}} and also called "Kusanagi." However, both shrines are said to be related to the legend of Yamato Takeru's fire disaster, and one theory suggests that this is where the legend of the fire disaster occurred.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}In addition to this place (Kusanagi, Suruga Province), there is a theory that the place where Yamato Takeru's fire disaster is said to have been said is compared to Ono, Sagami Province, and Yaizu, Suruga Province {{Harv|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. . === History === === Founding === [[File:Hyotanzuka-kofun (Shizuoka).JPG|thumb|200px|right|Hyotanzuka Tumulus (Yada, Shimizu Ward)
{{Smaller|The main tomb of the Nishinohara kofun group located at the northern foot of Mt. Udo. Located near Kusanagi Shrine. }}]] According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in {{jalink|Emperor Keiko|Q329723}} in 1953 when the Emperor visited places related to {{jalink|Yamato Takeru|Q461258|Nihon Takeru}}, and the Emperor arrived on September 20th to enshrine the spirit of Nihon Takeru. In addition, the Emperor dedicated the {{jalink|Kusanagi Sword}} to our company as {{jalink|Shintai|Q327532}}, and this Kusanagi Sword was discovered in the first year of {{jalink|Shudori|Q777413}} ({{ja It is also said that it was moved to {{jalink|Atsuta Shrine|Q482065}} by the imperial order of {{jalink|Emperor Tenmu|Q335181}} in 686|Q30619}}.. Also, according to the company's legend, the company was originally located in a place called ``Tennoubara'' in Kusanagi 1-chome, Shimizu Ward. It is not clear when it was moved to its current location, but one theory is that it was built in {{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}}18 ({{jalink|1590|Q6764}}).Based on historical research, as mentioned above, the founding of our company is said to have a great deal to do with the legend of Yamato Takeru. In the {{jalink|Shizuoka City|Q174691}} area, there are large-scale burial mounds such as {{jalink|Yatsuyama Tumulus|Q18460128}} (total length 110 meters) in {{jalink|Aoi Ward|Q1131483}} and {{jalink|Shimizu The {{jalink|Miikedaira Tumulus|Q107504282}} (total length 65 meters) in Ku|Q79385}} remains, and it is speculated that the subordination of the powers that built these tombs is behind the legend {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. It is assumed that the chief burial mounds in this area have changed over the course of ``Shimizu-ku Ibara and Yatsuyama Hills (early 4th century) → Sena Hills (middle 5th century) → North foot of Mt. Udo (late 6th century)'' {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods) |1987}}{{Sfn|Nishinohara Tumulus Group (Taira)|1979}} Among these, {{jalink|Kusanagi Shrine|Q11368944}} is located near Ibara in Shimizu-ku, and our company is located at the northern foot of Mt. Udo. There is also a theory that the name ``Kusanagi'' comes from a place name related to slash-and-burn cultivation {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. It has also been pointed out that the legend of Yamato Takeru lighting fires is a well-known method for preventing the spread of wildfires, and it is assumed that the legend of Yamato Takeru's fire disaster was established based on the preceding slash-and-burn place names {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. Before the legend of Yamato Takeru was established, it is believed that the shrine was the god of the mountains and the god of slash-and-burn crops, or the god of water since it was located in a valley {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. === Outline === {{jalink|extension (Era name)|Q1327274|Extended}} "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932| In the Shinmeicho}}, ``Kusanagi Shrine'' is written in {{jalink|Suruga Province|Q864170}}{{jalink|Arido District}}, and it is listed as {{jalink|Shikiuchi}}{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. It was worshiped by samurai families due to its connection with the Yamato Takeru tradition, and was worshiped for {{jalink|Tenkei|Q1327340}} years ({{jalink|877 years|Q23905}}-{{jalink|885 years| Q24894}}) says that an offering was made to pacify the {{jalink|Johei Tenkyo Rebellion#Taira no Masakado Rebellion|Q2975322|Taira no Masakado Rebellion}} {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}.In the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}}, the shogun {{jalink|Eikyo|Q1193059}}4 ({{jalink|1432|Q6349}}) {{jalink|Yoshitaka|Q11464813}}, who followed |Ashikaga Yoshinori|Q239170}} to Suruga Shimoki, wrote about Kusanagi Shrine {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. In {{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}}8 ({{jalink|1580|Q6712}}), a fishing lantern and a crocodile mouth (both cultural properties designated by Shizuoka City) were dedicated to the shrine by the petitioner, Moriminbu Dayu.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. In the 10th year of Tensho ({{jalink|1582|Q6719}}), felling of bamboo and trees was prohibited, and in the 18th year of Tensho ({{jalink|1590|Q6764}}), 18 koku of Kusanagi-go was granted as shrine territory by Daisuke Minbu, a social worker {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. This shrine territory was also relieved by Akira Yokotamura in {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}4 ({{jalink|1599|Q6803}}) {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. The shrine territory ({{jalink|shuinji}}) in the Edo period was 50 koku {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}.After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}3 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}), in {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534}} It was ranked as {{jalink|gosha|Q134917316}} and was promoted to {{jalink|prefectural shrine}} on July 24, 1879|1912}}. === God level === *Shogoi Lower Rank (``Suruga Province Shinto Name Book'') - Written as ``Kusanagi Tenjin.'' === Precincts === The shrine grounds are located in the valley of the upper reaches of the Kusanagi River at the northern foot of Mt. Udo.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. The area is 0.7 hectares. The trunk of the camphor tree that stands within the shrine grounds has withered and only the outer skin remains, but it is an old tree that is 6 meters high and 25 meters in circumference and is said to be over 1,000 years old, and has been designated as a natural monument by Shizuoka City. The flat land near {{jalink|Kusanagi Station (JR Tokai)|Q871079|Kusanagi Station}} is called "Tennoubara" . It is said that Kusanagi Shrine was once located on this land of Emperor Hara, and the place is still known as the "Old Palace." . File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) keidai.JPG|Precincts File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) zuishinmon.JPG|ZushinmonFile:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) kusu.JPG|Big Camphor (Shizuoka City designated natural monument) File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) torii.JPG|Torii File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) furumiya.JPG|Furumiya === Otorii === [[File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) ichinotorii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Ichino Torii]] The concrete "Ichi-no-Torii" gate, which was built across the city road near Kusanagi Station, had existed since the 1950s, but was removed in 2020. ==== Removal ==== In 2019, it was confirmed that some surface damage had occurred. Since around 2018, Kusanagi Shrine has received inquiries about its management from local residents and parishioners, and when the shrine checked again, it was discovered that the property was not owned by the shrine, and past records of the city and prefecture had to be checked. As a result, in 1975, when Shizuoka Prefecture carried out widening work on the city road as part of the land readjustment project, the prefecture rebuilt the road in the same location to match the new road width as compensation for restoring it to its original condition, but it was once again revealed that the owner was unknown at that time. In other words, it has existed as a local symbol since the 1970s without any maintenance or maintenance. Shizuoka/Kusanagi Shrine approach entrance|date=2020-01-08|publisher=AtS Shizuoka Shimbun SBS}}.Due to the risk of collapse due to deterioration, surrounding residents requested its removal, and Shizuoka City issued a public notice ordering the owner to remove it from March 24, 2020 to June 15 of the same year. We will issue a public notice ordering the owners of the large torii gate and two stone monuments on the South Main Line to remove them! ]}} (Shizuoka City Press Materials, March 24, 2020) Since the owner did not come forward, the city announced a plan to carry out a compulsory summary execution at a cost of 11 million yen, including the cost of removing the two stone monuments next to the torii gate and relocating surrounding telephone and electric cables.{{PDFlink|[https://www.city.shizuoka.lg.jp/000864003.pdf Torii gates, etc. on the South Main Line will be removed by proxy execution]}} (Shizuoka City press material, August 17, 2020), and were removed within the same year.According to research and interviews by Mainichi Shimbun reporters, The evening edition social section of the Tokyo edition of October 31, 2020 (p. 9), articles were found in local newspapers ({{jalink|Shizuoka Shimbun|Q904670}}) that corroborated that the building was built with funding from many local volunteers, with no direct involvement of the shrine, and that is probably why the ownership and ownership became unclear. A similar and well-known example is the case where the Shiba {{jalink|Zojoji Temple #Old Main Gate ``Daimon''|Q249139|Zojoji Daimon}} fell out of the capital's property inventory for some reason, making it impossible to confirm its owner. , there is a comment that in religious buildings, the ownership and management are often unclear due to the idea that they belong to everyone, and in this case, it was left unclear when the building was rebuilt in 1975, which led to its demolition. === Setsusha === There are 9 shrines on the south side of the main shrine and 9 shrines on the north side of the shrine. File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) kita-massha.JPG|Kitaside Suesha File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) minami-massha.JPG|South side shrine === Festival === The {{jalink|regular festival|Q11385469}} is held on September 20th, and this day is said to be the day the shrine was founded by Emperor Keiko. On that day, {{jalink|fireworks|Q127933}}, which evolved from {{jalink|fireworks|Q127933}}, called "{{jalink|dragon force|Q11679206}} (meteor) fireworks" will be launched. These fireworks are said to have continued for the {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}} years ({{jalink|1854|Q7670}}-{{jalink|1860|Q7691}}), and are designated as an intangible folk cultural property by Shizuoka Prefecture. === Cultural assets === === Shizuoka Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * Kusanagi Shrine Ryusei Fireworks - Designated on March 24, 2003. === Cultural property designated by Shizuoka City === *Tangible cultural property ** Fishing lantern (craft) - Dedicated by Morimbu Tayu in {{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}}8 ({{jalink|1580|Q6712}}) at the end of the {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}}. Designated on September 17, 1960. ** Waniguchi (craft) - Dedicated by Morimbu Tayu in 1580 at the end of the Muromachi period. Designated on September 17, 1960. * Natural monument ** Big Camphor - Designated on September 17, 1960. === Local information === '''Location''' * {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}{{jalink|Shizuoka City|Q174691}}{{jalink|Shimizu Ward|Q79385}}{{jalink|Kusanagi (Shizuoka City)|Q11618222|Kusanagi}}349 '''Access''' * From {{jalink|Tokai Passenger Railway|Q513679}} (JR Tokai) {{jalink|Tokaido Line (Shizuoka Area)|Q11527981|Tokaido Main Line}} {{jalink|Kusanagi Station (JR Tokai)|Q871079|Kusanagi Station}} ** Bus: Take the Shizutetsu Just Line (Miho Kusanagi Line) and get off at the "Kusanagi Jinja-mae" bus stop (about 7 minutes on the bus, a short walk after getting off) '''Surroundings''' * Hyōtanzuka Tumulus (Nishinohara No. 1 Tumulus, {{Wiki coordinates|34|59|53.74|N|138|26|40.83|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|location|name=Hyotanzuka Tumulus}}) - A keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of approximately 45.8 meters {{Sfn|Nishinohara Tumulus Group (Taira)|1979}}. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="Note"}} '''Source''' {{reflist|2}} === References === * Shrine history book * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Meiji Shrine Library|author=|year=1912|chapter=Kusanagi Shrine|title={{jalink|Meiji Shrine Library|Q11486103}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Library|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine Library|1912}}}} ** [{{NDLDC|1088244/883}} "Meiji Shrine Journal Materials Fuken Gosha Volume 1"] (National Diet Library Digital Collection) Reference frames 883-884. * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Kenichi Tanikawa|editor-link=Kenichi Tanikawa|author=Kanichi Nomoto|authorlink=Kanichi Nomoto|year=1987|chapter=Kusanagi Shrine|title=Japanese Gods -Shrines and Sacred Sites- 10 Tokai|publisher={{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}|isbn=4560022208|ref={{Harvid|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title=Japanese Historical Place Name System 22 Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}|isbn=4582490220|ref=}}** {{Wikicite|reference="Kusanagi Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Nishinohara Tumulus Group"|ref={{Harvid|Nishinohara Tumulus Group (Taira)|1979}}}} ===External link=== [https://kusanagijinjya.jp/ Kusanagi Shrine official website] === Related items === {{Commonscat|Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)}} * {{jalink|Amamuragumo Ken|Q14944}} (Kusanagi Ken) * {{jalink|Kusanagi Shrine|Q11368944}} * {{jalink|Yaizu Shrine|Q3571342}} * {{jalink|Katori Shrine|Q372380}} - {{jalink|SMAP|Q1138250}} A shrine with the same name as the member. {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kusanaki Shinsiya}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kusanagi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kusanagi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Kusanagi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suruga Province]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:清水区の歴史]] [[Category:駿河国の式内社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧県社]] [[Category:ヤマトタケル伝説]] [[Category:清水区の建築物]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqq]]

City, Mie Prefecture|Kusanagi Shrine}} {{Shrine |Name = Kusanagi Shrine |Image = [[File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) haiden.JPG|280px]]
House of worship |Location = {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}{{jalink|Shizuoka City|Q174691}}{{jalink|Shimizu Ward|Q79385}}{{jalink|Kusanagi (Shizuoka City)|Q11618222|Kusanagi}}349 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 steps degrees minutes seconds|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kusanagi Shrine}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Yamato Takeru|Q461258|Japan Takeru}} |Divinity = |Company rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
Old {{jalink|Prefectural company}} |Foundation = (tradition) 12th {{jalink|Emperor Keiko|Q329723}}53 |Main shrine = {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{jalink|September 20th|Q2892}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Kusanagi Shrine|coordinates={{coord2|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E}}}} }} {{List of coordinates}} '''Kusanagi Shrine'' (Kusanagi Shrine) is located in {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} located in (Shizuoka City)|Q11618222|Kusanagi}}. {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534|Old company rank}} is {{jalink|Prefectural company}}. === Enshrined God === The enshrined deity is one of the followingShrine Historysho. . * '''{{jalink|Yamato Takeru|Q461258|Nippon Takeru}}''' (Yamato Takeru Mikoto) According to the legends of "{{jalink|Kojiki|Q813031}}" and "{{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}," during Yamato Takeru's eastern expedition, bandits They tried to set the field where Totakeru was on fire and burn it to death, but Yamatotakeru escaped by mowing down the grass with {{jalink|Amamuragumo Sword|Q14944}} and setting fire to it. For this reason, the Ten Murakumo sword is called the ``Kusanagi sword'' {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. To the northeast of our company is {{jalink|Kusanagi Shrine|Q11368944}} (Yamakiri, Shimizu Ward, Shizuoka City), which is also known as {{jalink|Shikiuchi}} and also called "Kusanagi." However, both shrines are said to be related to the legend of Yamato Takeru's fire disaster, and one theory suggests that this is where the legend of the fire disaster occurred.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}In addition to this place (Kusanagi, Suruga Province), there is a theory that the place where Yamato Takeru's fire disaster is said to have been said is compared to Ono, Sagami Province, and Yaizu, Suruga Province {{Harv|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. . === History === === Founding === [[File:Hyotanzuka-kofun (Shizuoka).JPG|thumb|200px|right|Hyotanzuka Tumulus (Yada, Shimizu Ward)
{{Smaller|The main tomb of the Nishinohara kofun group located at the northern foot of Mt. Udo. Located near Kusanagi Shrine. }}]] According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in {{jalink|Emperor Keiko|Q329723}} in 1953 when the Emperor visited places related to {{jalink|Yamato Takeru|Q461258|Nihon Takeru}}, and the Emperor arrived on September 20th to enshrine the spirit of Nihon Takeru. In addition, the Emperor dedicated the {{jalink|Kusanagi Sword}} to our company as {{jalink|Shintai|Q327532}}, and this Kusanagi Sword was discovered in the first year of {{jalink|Shudori|Q777413}} ({{ja It is also said that it was moved to {{jalink|Atsuta Shrine|Q482065}} by the imperial order of {{jalink|Emperor Tenmu|Q335181}} in 686|Q30619}}.. Also, according to the company's legend, the company was originally located in a place called ``Tennoubara'' in Kusanagi 1-chome, Shimizu Ward. It is not clear when it was moved to its current location, but one theory is that it was built in {{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}}18 ({{jalink|1590|Q6764}}).Based on historical research, as mentioned above, the founding of our company is said to have a great deal to do with the legend of Yamato Takeru. In the {{jalink|Shizuoka City|Q174691}} area, there are large-scale burial mounds such as {{jalink|Yatsuyama Tumulus|Q18460128}} (total length 110 meters) in {{jalink|Aoi Ward|Q1131483}} and {{jalink|Shimizu The {{jalink|Miikedaira Tumulus|Q107504282}} (total length 65 meters) in Ku|Q79385}} remains, and it is speculated that the subordination of the powers that built these tombs is behind the legend {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. It is assumed that the chief burial mounds in this area have changed over the course of ``Shimizu-ku Ibara and Yatsuyama Hills (early 4th century) → Sena Hills (middle 5th century) → North foot of Mt. Udo (late 6th century)'' {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods) |1987}}{{Sfn|Nishinohara Tumulus Group (Taira)|1979}} Among these, {{jalink|Kusanagi Shrine|Q11368944}} is located near Ibara in Shimizu-ku, and our company is located at the northern foot of Mt. Udo. There is also a theory that the name ``Kusanagi'' comes from a place name related to slash-and-burn cultivation {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. It has also been pointed out that the legend of Yamato Takeru lighting fires is a well-known method for preventing the spread of wildfires, and it is assumed that the legend of Yamato Takeru's fire disaster was established based on the preceding slash-and-burn place names {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. Before the legend of Yamato Takeru was established, it is believed that the shrine was the god of the mountains and the god of slash-and-burn crops, or the god of water since it was located in a valley {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. === Outline === {{jalink|extension (Era name)|Q1327274|Extended}} "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932| In the Shinmeicho}}, ``Kusanagi Shrine'' is written in {{jalink|Suruga Province|Q864170}}{{jalink|Arido District}}, and it is listed as {{jalink|Shikiuchi}}{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. It was worshiped by samurai families due to its connection with the Yamato Takeru tradition, and was worshiped for {{jalink|Tenkei|Q1327340}} years ({{jalink|877 years|Q23905}}-{{jalink|885 years| Q24894}}) says that an offering was made to pacify the {{jalink|Johei Tenkyo Rebellion#Taira no Masakado Rebellion|Q2975322|Taira no Masakado Rebellion}} {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}.In the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}}, the shogun {{jalink|Eikyo|Q1193059}}4 ({{jalink|1432|Q6349}}) {{jalink|Yoshitaka|Q11464813}}, who followed |Ashikaga Yoshinori|Q239170}} to Suruga Shimoki, wrote about Kusanagi Shrine {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. In {{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}}8 ({{jalink|1580|Q6712}}), a fishing lantern and a crocodile mouth (both cultural properties designated by Shizuoka City) were dedicated to the shrine by the petitioner, Moriminbu Dayu.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. In the 10th year of Tensho ({{jalink|1582|Q6719}}), felling of bamboo and trees was prohibited, and in the 18th year of Tensho ({{jalink|1590|Q6764}}), 18 koku of Kusanagi-go was granted as shrine territory by Daisuke Minbu, a social worker {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. This shrine territory was also relieved by Akira Yokotamura in {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}4 ({{jalink|1599|Q6803}}) {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. The shrine territory ({{jalink|shuinji}}) in the Edo period was 50 koku {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}.After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}3 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}), in {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534}} It was ranked as {{jalink|gosha|Q134917316}} and was promoted to {{jalink|prefectural shrine}} on July 24, 1879|1912}}. === God level === *Shogoi Lower Rank (``Suruga Province Shinto Name Book'') - Written as ``Kusanagi Tenjin.'' === Precincts === The shrine grounds are located in the valley of the upper reaches of the Kusanagi River at the northern foot of Mt. Udo.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. The area is 0.7 hectares. The trunk of the camphor tree that stands within the shrine grounds has withered and only the outer skin remains, but it is an old tree that is 6 meters high and 25 meters in circumference and is said to be over 1,000 years old, and has been designated as a natural monument by Shizuoka City. The flat land near {{jalink|Kusanagi Station (JR Tokai)|Q871079|Kusanagi Station}} is called "Tennoubara" . It is said that Kusanagi Shrine was once located on this land of Emperor Hara, and the place is still known as the "Old Palace." . File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) keidai.JPG|Precincts File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) zuishinmon.JPG|ZushinmonFile:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) kusu.JPG|Big Camphor (Shizuoka City designated natural monument) File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) torii.JPG|Torii File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) furumiya.JPG|Furumiya === Otorii === [[File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) ichinotorii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Ichino Torii]] The concrete "Ichi-no-Torii" gate, which was built across the city road near Kusanagi Station, had existed since the 1950s, but was removed in 2020. ==== Removal ==== In 2019, it was confirmed that some surface damage had occurred. Since around 2018, Kusanagi Shrine has received inquiries about its management from local residents and parishioners, and when the shrine checked again, it was discovered that the property was not owned by the shrine, and past records of the city and prefecture had to be checked. As a result, in 1975, when Shizuoka Prefecture carried out widening work on the city road as part of the land readjustment project, the prefecture rebuilt the road in the same location to match the new road width as compensation for restoring it to its original condition, but it was once again revealed that the owner was unknown at that time. In other words, it has existed as a local symbol since the 1970s without any maintenance or maintenance. Shizuoka/Kusanagi Shrine approach entrance|date=2020-01-08|publisher=AtS Shizuoka Shimbun SBS}}.Due to the risk of collapse due to deterioration, surrounding residents requested its removal, and Shizuoka City issued a public notice ordering the owner to remove it from March 24, 2020 to June 15 of the same year. We will issue a public notice ordering the owners of the large torii gate and two stone monuments on the South Main Line to remove them! ]}} (Shizuoka City Press Materials, March 24, 2020) Since the owner did not come forward, the city announced a plan to carry out a compulsory summary execution at a cost of 11 million yen, including the cost of removing the two stone monuments next to the torii gate and relocating surrounding telephone and electric cables.{{PDFlink|[https://www.city.shizuoka.lg.jp/000864003.pdf Torii gates, etc. on the South Main Line will be removed by proxy execution]}} (Shizuoka City press material, August 17, 2020), and were removed within the same year.According to research and interviews by Mainichi Shimbun reporters, The evening edition social section of the Tokyo edition of October 31, 2020 (p. 9), articles were found in local newspapers ({{jalink|Shizuoka Shimbun|Q904670}}) that corroborated that the building was built with funding from many local volunteers, with no direct involvement of the shrine, and that is probably why the ownership and ownership became unclear. A similar and well-known example is the case where the Shiba {{jalink|Zojoji Temple #Old Main Gate ``Daimon''|Q249139|Zojoji Daimon}} fell out of the capital's property inventory for some reason, making it impossible to confirm its owner. , there is a comment that in religious buildings, the ownership and management are often unclear due to the idea that they belong to everyone, and in this case, it was left unclear when the building was rebuilt in 1975, which led to its demolition. === Setsusha === There are 9 shrines on the south side of the main shrine and 9 shrines on the north side of the shrine. File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) kita-massha.JPG|Kitaside Suesha File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) minami-massha.JPG|South side shrine === Festival === The {{jalink|regular festival|Q11385469}} is held on September 20th, and this day is said to be the day the shrine was founded by Emperor Keiko. On that day, {{jalink|fireworks|Q127933}}, which evolved from {{jalink|fireworks|Q127933}}, called "{{jalink|dragon force|Q11679206}} (meteor) fireworks" will be launched. These fireworks are said to have continued for the {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}} years ({{jalink|1854|Q7670}}-{{jalink|1860|Q7691}}), and are designated as an intangible folk cultural property by Shizuoka Prefecture. === Cultural assets === === Shizuoka Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * Kusanagi Shrine Ryusei Fireworks - Designated on March 24, 2003. === Cultural property designated by Shizuoka City === *Tangible cultural property ** Fishing lantern (craft) - Dedicated by Morimbu Tayu in {{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}}8 ({{jalink|1580|Q6712}}) at the end of the {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}}. Designated on September 17, 1960. ** Waniguchi (craft) - Dedicated by Morimbu Tayu in 1580 at the end of the Muromachi period. Designated on September 17, 1960. * Natural monument ** Big Camphor - Designated on September 17, 1960. === Local information === '''Location''' * {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}{{jalink|Shizuoka City|Q174691}}{{jalink|Shimizu Ward|Q79385}}{{jalink|Kusanagi (Shizuoka City)|Q11618222|Kusanagi}}349 '''Access''' * From {{jalink|Tokai Passenger Railway|Q513679}} (JR Tokai) {{jalink|Tokaido Line (Shizuoka Area)|Q11527981|Tokaido Main Line}} {{jalink|Kusanagi Station (JR Tokai)|Q871079|Kusanagi Station}} ** Bus: Take the Shizutetsu Just Line (Miho Kusanagi Line) and get off at the "Kusanagi Jinja-mae" bus stop (about 7 minutes on the bus, a short walk after getting off) '''Surroundings''' * Hyōtanzuka Tumulus (Nishinohara No. 1 Tumulus, {{Wiki coordinates|34|59|53.74|N|138|26|40.83|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|location|name=Hyotanzuka Tumulus}}) - Old {{jalink|Prefectural company}} |Foundation = (tradition) 12th {{jalink|Emperor Keiko|Q329723}}53 |Main shrine = {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{jalink|September 20th|Q2892}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Kusanagi Shrine|coordinates={{coord2|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E}}}} }} {{List of coordinates}} '''Kusanagi Shrine'' (Kusanagi Shrine) is located in {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} located in (Shizuoka City)|Q11618222|Kusanagi}}. {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534|Old company rank}} is {{jalink|Prefectural company}}. === Enshrined God === The enshrined deity is one of the followingShrine Historysho. . * '''{{jalink|Yamato Takeru|Q461258|Nippon Takeru}}''' (Yamato Takeru Mikoto) According to the legends of "{{jalink|Kojiki|Q813031}}" and "{{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}," during Yamato Takeru's eastern expedition, bandits They tried to set the field where Totakeru was on fire and burn it to death, but Yamatotakeru escaped by mowing down the grass with {{jalink|Amamuragumo Sword|Q14944}} and setting fire to it. For this reason, the Ten Murakumo sword is called the ``Kusanagi sword'' {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. To the northeast of our company is {{jalink|Kusanagi Shrine|Q11368944}} (Yamakiri, Shimizu Ward, Shizuoka City), which is also known as {{jalink|Shikiuchi}} and also called "Kusanagi." However, both shrines are said to be related to the legend of Yamato Takeru's fire disaster, and one theory suggests that this is where the legend of the fire disaster occurred.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}In addition to this place (Kusanagi, Suruga Province), there is a theory that the place where Yamato Takeru's fire disaster is said to have been said is compared to Ono, Sagami Province, and Yaizu, Suruga Province {{Harv|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. . === History === === Founding === [[File:Hyotanzuka-kofun (Shizuoka).JPG|thumb|200px|right|Hyotanzuka Tumulus (Yada, Shimizu Ward)
{{Smaller|The main tomb of the Nishinohara kofun group located at the northern foot of Mt. Udo. Located near Kusanagi Shrine. }}]] According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in {{jalink|Emperor Keiko|Q329723}} in 1953 when the Emperor visited places related to {{jalink|Yamato Takeru|Q461258|Nihon Takeru}}, and the Emperor arrived on September 20th to enshrine the spirit of Nihon Takeru. In addition, the Emperor dedicated the {{jalink|Kusanagi Sword}} to our company as {{jalink|Shintai|Q327532}}, and this Kusanagi Sword was discovered in the first year of {{jalink|Shudori|Q777413}} ({{ja It is also said that it was moved to {{jalink|Atsuta Shrine|Q482065}} by the imperial order of {{jalink|Emperor Tenmu|Q335181}} in 686|Q30619}}.. Also, according to the company's legend, the company was originally located in a place called ``Tennoubara'' in Kusanagi 1-chome, Shimizu Ward. It is not clear when it was moved to its current location, but one theory is that it was built in {{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}}18 ({{jalink|1590|Q6764}}).Based on historical research, as mentioned above, the founding of our company is said to have a great deal to do with the legend of Yamato Takeru. In the {{jalink|Shizuoka City|Q174691}} area, there are large-scale burial mounds such as {{jalink|Yatsuyama Tumulus|Q18460128}} (total length 110 meters) in {{jalink|Aoi Ward|Q1131483}} and {{jalink|Shimizu The {{jalink|Miikedaira Tumulus|Q107504282}} (total length 65 meters) in Ku|Q79385}} remains, and it is speculated that the subordination of the powers that built these tombs is behind the legend {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. It is assumed that the chief burial mounds in this area have changed over the course of ``Shimizu-ku Ibara and Yatsuyama Hills (early 4th century) → Sena Hills (middle 5th century) → North foot of Mt. Udo (late 6th century)'' {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods) |1987}}{{Sfn|Nishinohara Tumulus Group (Taira)|1979}} Among these, {{jalink|Kusanagi Shrine|Q11368944}} is located near Ibara in Shimizu-ku, and our company is located at the northern foot of Mt. Udo. There is also a theory that the name ``Kusanagi'' comes from a place name related to slash-and-burn cultivation {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. It has also been pointed out that the legend of Yamato Takeru lighting fires is a well-known method for preventing the spread of wildfires, and it is assumed that the legend of Yamato Takeru's fire disaster was established based on the preceding slash-and-burn place names {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. Before the legend of Yamato Takeru was established, it is believed that the shrine was the god of the mountains and the god of slash-and-burn crops, or the god of water since it was located in a valley {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. === Outline === {{jalink|extension (Era name)|Q1327274|Extended}} "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932| In the Shinmeicho}}, ``Kusanagi Shrine'' is written in {{jalink|Suruga Province|Q864170}}{{jalink|Arido District}}, and it is listed as {{jalink|Shikiuchi}}{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. It was worshiped by samurai families due to its connection with the Yamato Takeru tradition, and was worshiped for {{jalink|Tenkei|Q1327340}} years ({{jalink|877 years|Q23905}}-{{jalink|885 years| Q24894}}) says that an offering was made to pacify the {{jalink|Johei Tenkyo Rebellion#Taira no Masakado Rebellion|Q2975322|Taira no Masakado Rebellion}} {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}.In the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}}, the shogun {{jalink|Eikyo|Q1193059}}4 ({{jalink|1432|Q6349}}) {{jalink|Yoshitaka|Q11464813}}, who followed |Ashikaga Yoshinori|Q239170}} to Suruga Shimoki, wrote about Kusanagi Shrine {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. In {{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}}8 ({{jalink|1580|Q6712}}), a fishing lantern and a crocodile mouth (both cultural properties designated by Shizuoka City) were dedicated to the shrine by the petitioner, Moriminbu Dayu.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. In the 10th year of Tensho ({{jalink|1582|Q6719}}), felling of bamboo and trees was prohibited, and in the 18th year of Tensho ({{jalink|1590|Q6764}}), 18 koku of Kusanagi-go was granted as shrine territory by Daisuke Minbu, a social worker {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. This shrine territory was also relieved by Akira Yokotamura in {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}4 ({{jalink|1599|Q6803}}) {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. The shrine territory ({{jalink|shuinji}}) in the Edo period was 50 koku {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}.After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}3 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}), in {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534}} It was ranked as {{jalink|gosha|Q134917316}} and was promoted to {{jalink|prefectural shrine}} on July 24, 1879|1912}}. === God level === *Shogoi Lower Rank (``Suruga Province Shinto Name Book'') - Written as ``Kusanagi Tenjin.'' === Precincts === The shrine grounds are located in the valley of the upper reaches of the Kusanagi River at the northern foot of Mt. Udo.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. The area is 0.7 hectares. The trunk of the camphor tree that stands within the shrine grounds has withered and only the outer skin remains, but it is an old tree that is 6 meters high and 25 meters in circumference and is said to be over 1,000 years old, and has been designated as a natural monument by Shizuoka City. The flat land near {{jalink|Kusanagi Station (JR Tokai)|Q871079|Kusanagi Station}} is called "Tennoubara" . It is said that Kusanagi Shrine was once located on this land of Emperor Hara, and the place is still known as the "Old Palace." . File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) keidai.JPG|Precincts File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) zuishinmon.JPG|ZushinmonFile:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) kusu.JPG|Big Camphor (Shizuoka City designated natural monument) File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) torii.JPG|Torii File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) furumiya.JPG|Furumiya === Otorii === [[File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) ichinotorii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Ichino Torii]] The concrete "Ichi-no-Torii" gate, which was built across the city road near Kusanagi Station, had existed since the 1950s, but was removed in 2020. ==== Removal ==== In 2019, it was confirmed that some surface damage had occurred. Since around 2018, Kusanagi Shrine has received inquiries about its management from local residents and parishioners, and when the shrine checked again, it was discovered that the property was not owned by the shrine, and past records of the city and prefecture had to be checked. As a result, in 1975, when Shizuoka Prefecture carried out widening work on the city road as part of the land readjustment project, the prefecture rebuilt the road in the same location to match the new road width as compensation for restoring it to its original condition, but it was once again revealed that the owner was unknown at that time. In other words, it has existed as a local symbol since the 1970s without any maintenance or maintenance. Shizuoka/Kusanagi Shrine approach entrance|date=2020-01-08|publisher=AtS Shizuoka Shimbun SBS}}.Due to the risk of collapse due to deterioration, surrounding residents requested its removal, and Shizuoka City issued a public notice ordering the owner to remove it from March 24, 2020 to June 15 of the same year. We will issue a public notice ordering the owners of the large torii gate and two stone monuments on the South Main Line to remove them! ]}} (Shizuoka City Press Materials, March 24, 2020) Since the owner did not come forward, the city announced a plan to carry out a compulsory summary execution at a cost of 11 million yen, including the cost of removing the two stone monuments next to the torii gate and relocating surrounding telephone and electric cables.{{PDFlink|[https://www.city.shizuoka.lg.jp/000864003.pdf Torii gates, etc. on the South Main Line will be removed by proxy execution]}} (Shizuoka City press material, August 17, 2020), and were removed within the same year.According to research and interviews by Mainichi Shimbun reporters, The evening edition social section of the Tokyo edition of October 31, 2020 (p. 9), articles were found in local newspapers ({{jalink|Shizuoka Shimbun|Q904670}}) that corroborated that the building was built with funding from many local volunteers, with no direct involvement of the shrine, and that is probably why the ownership and ownership became unclear. A similar and well-known example is the case where the Shiba {{jalink|Zojoji Temple #Old Main Gate ``Daimon''|Q249139|Zojoji Daimon}} fell out of the capital's property inventory for some reason, making it impossible to confirm its owner. , there is a comment that in religious buildings, the ownership and management are often unclear due to the idea that they belong to everyone, and in this case, it was left unclear when the building was rebuilt in 1975, which led to its demolition. === Setsusha === There are 9 shrines on the south side of the main shrine and 9 shrines on the north side of the shrine. File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) kita-massha.JPG|Kitaside Suesha File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) minami-massha.JPG|South side shrine === Festival === The {{jalink|regular festival|Q11385469}} is held on September 20th, and this day is said to be the day the shrine was founded by Emperor Keiko. On that day, {{jalink|fireworks|Q127933}}, which evolved from {{jalink|fireworks|Q127933}}, called "{{jalink|dragon force|Q11679206}} (meteor) fireworks" will be launched. These fireworks are said to have continued for the {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}} years ({{jalink|1854|Q7670}}-{{jalink|1860|Q7691}}), and are designated as an intangible folk cultural property by Shizuoka Prefecture. === Cultural assets === === Shizuoka Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * Kusanagi Shrine Ryusei Fireworks - Designated on March 24, 2003. === Cultural property designated by Shizuoka City === *Tangible cultural property ** Fishing lantern (craft) - Dedicated by Morimbu Tayu in {{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}}8 ({{jalink|1580|Q6712}}) at the end of the {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}}. Designated on September 17, 1960. ** Waniguchi (craft) - Dedicated by Morimbu Tayu in 1580 at the end of the Muromachi period.
