Shizuoka)}} [[JA:草薙神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11618229}} == Japanese content == {{Otheruses|Shrine in Kusanagi, Shimizu Ward, Shizuoka City|Shrine in Yamakiri, Shimizu Ward with the same reading|Kusanagi Shrine|Shrine in Ise City, Mie Prefecture|Kusanagi Shrine}} {{Shrine |Name = Kusanagi Shrine |Image = [[File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) haiden.JPG|280px]]
House of worship |Location = {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320}}{{ill|lt=Shizuoka City|Q174691|WD=Q174691}}{{ill|lt=Shimizu Ward|Q79385|WD=Q79385}}{{ill|lt=Kusanagi (Shizuoka City)|Q11618222|WD=Q11618222|lt=Kusanagi}}349 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 steps degrees minutes seconds|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kusanagi Shrine}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Japan Takeru}} |Divinity = |Company rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}} |Foundation = (tradition) 12th {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|WD=Q329723}}53 |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=September 20th|Q2892|WD=Q2892}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Kusanagi Shrine|coordinates={{coord2|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E}}}} }} {{List of coordinates}} '''Kusanagi Shrine'' (Kusanagi Shrine) is located in {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in (Shizuoka City)|Q11618222|Kusanagi}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} and {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old company rank}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}}. === Enshrined God === The enshrined deity is one of the followingShrine Historysho. . * '''{{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nippon Takeru}}''' (Yamato Takeru Mikoto) According to the legends of "{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031}}" and "{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}}," during Yamato Takeru's eastern expedition, bandits They tried to set the field where Totakeru was on fire and burn it to death, but Yamatotakeru escaped by mowing down the grass with {{ill|lt=Amamuragumo Sword|Q14944|WD=Q14944}} and setting fire to it. For this reason, the Ten Murakumo sword is called the ``Kusanagi sword'' {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. To the northeast of our company is {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Shrine|Q11368944|WD=Q11368944}} (Yamakiri, Shimizu Ward, Shizuoka City), which is also known as {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} and also called "Kusanagi." However, both shrines are said to be related to the legend of Yamato Takeru's fire disaster, and one theory suggests that this is where the legend of the fire disaster occurred.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}In addition to this place (Kusanagi, Suruga Province), there is a theory that the place where Yamato Takeru's fire disaster is said to have been said is compared to Ono, Sagami Province, and Yaizu, Suruga Province {{Harv|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. . === History === === Founding === [[File:Hyotanzuka-kofun (Shizuoka).JPG|thumb|200px|right|Hyotanzuka Tumulus (Yada, Shimizu Ward)
{{Smaller|The main tomb of the Nishinohara kofun group located at the northern foot of Mt. Udo. Located near Kusanagi Shrine. }}]] According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} in 1953 when the Emperor visited places related to {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nihon Takeru}}, and the Emperor arrived on September 20th to enshrine the spirit of Nihon Takeru. In addition, the Emperor dedicated the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kusanagi Sword|1=UNKNOWN}} to our company as {{ill|lt=Shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532}}, and this Kusanagi Sword was discovered in the first year of {{ill|lt=Shudori|Q777413|WD=Q777413}} ({{ja It is also said that it was moved to {{ill|lt=Atsuta Shrine|Q482065|WD=Q482065}} by the imperial order of {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenmu|Q335181|WD=Q335181}} in 686|Q30619}}.. Also, according to the company's legend, the company was originally located in a place called ``Tennoubara'' in Kusanagi 1-chome, Shimizu Ward. It is not clear when it was moved to its current location, but one theory is that it was built in {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}}18 ({{ill|lt=1590|Q6764|WD=Q6764}}).Based on historical research, as mentioned above, the founding of our company is said to have a great deal to do with the legend of Yamato Takeru. In the {{ill|lt=Shizuoka City|Q174691|WD=Q174691}} area, there are large-scale burial mounds such as {{ill|lt=Yatsuyama Tumulus|Q18460128|WD=Q18460128}} (total length 110 meters) in {{ill|lt=Aoi Ward|Q1131483|WD=Q1131483}} and {{jalink|Shimizu The {{ill|lt=Miikedaira Tumulus|Q107504282|WD=Q107504282}} (total length 65 meters) in Ku|Q79385}} remains, and it is speculated that the subordination of the powers that built these tombs is behind the legend {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. It is assumed that the chief burial mounds in this area have changed over the course of ``Shimizu-ku Ibara and Yatsuyama Hills (early 4th century) → Sena Hills (middle 5th century) → North foot of Mt. Udo (late 6th century)'' {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods) |1987}}{{Sfn|Nishinohara Tumulus Group (Taira)|1979}} Among these, {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Shrine|Q11368944|WD=Q11368944}} is located near Ibara in Shimizu-ku, and our company is located at the northern foot of Mt. Udo. There is also a theory that the name ``Kusanagi'' comes from a place name related to slash-and-burn cultivation {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. It has also been pointed out that the legend of Yamato Takeru lighting fires is a well-known method for preventing the spread of wildfires, and it is assumed that the legend of Yamato Takeru's fire disaster was established based on the preceding slash-and-burn place names {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. Before the legend of Yamato Takeru was established, it is believed that the shrine was the god of the mountains and the god of slash-and-burn crops, or the god of water since it was located in a valley {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. === Outline === {{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended}} "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}) {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=In the Shinmeicho}}, ``Kusanagi Shrine'' is written in {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|WD=Q864170}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Arido District|1=UNKNOWN}}, and it is listed as {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}}{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. It was worshiped by samurai families due to its connection with the Yamato Takeru tradition, and was worshiped for {{ill|lt=Tenkei|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340}} years ({{ill|lt=877 years|Q23905|WD=Q23905}}-{{ill|lt=885 years|Q24894|WD=Q24894}}) says that an offering was made to pacify the {{ill|lt=Johei Tenkyo Rebellion#Taira no Masakado Rebellion|Q2975322|WD=Q2975322|lt=Taira no Masakado Rebellion}} {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}.In the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|WD=Q12554}}, the shogun {{ill|lt=Eikyo|Q1193059|WD=Q1193059}}4 ({{ill|lt=1432|Q6349|WD=Q6349}}) {{ill|lt=Yoshitaka|Q11464813|WD=Q11464813}}, who followed |Ashikaga Yoshinori|Q239170}} to Suruga Shimoki, wrote about Kusanagi Shrine {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. In {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}}8 ({{ill|lt=1580|Q6712|WD=Q6712}}), a fishing lantern and a crocodile mouth (both cultural properties designated by Shizuoka City) were dedicated to the shrine by the petitioner, Moriminbu Dayu.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. In the 10th year of Tensho ({{ill|lt=1582|Q6719|WD=Q6719}}), felling of bamboo and trees was prohibited, and in the 18th year of Tensho ({{ill|lt=1590|Q6764|WD=Q6764}}), 18 koku of Kusanagi-go was granted as shrine territory by Daisuke Minbu, a social worker {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. This shrine territory was also relieved by Akira Yokotamura in {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}4 ({{ill|lt=1599|Q6803|WD=Q6803}}) {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. The shrine territory ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shuinji|1=UNKNOWN}}) in the Edo period was 50 koku {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}.After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}3 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}), in {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}} It was ranked as {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} and was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226}} on July 24, 1879|1912}}. === God level === *Shogoi Lower Rank (``Suruga Province Shinto Name Book'') - Written as ``Kusanagi Tenjin.'' === Precincts === The shrine grounds are located in the valley of the upper reaches of the Kusanagi River at the northern foot of Mt. Udo.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. The area is 0.7 hectares. The trunk of the camphor tree that stands within the shrine grounds has withered and only the outer skin remains, but it is an old tree that is 6 meters high and 25 meters in circumference and is said to be over 1,000 years old, and has been designated as a natural monument by Shizuoka City. The flat land near {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Station (JR Tokai)|Q871079|WD=Q871079|lt=Kusanagi Station}} is called "Tennoubara" . It is said that Kusanagi Shrine was once located on this land of Emperor Hara, and the place is still known as the "Old Palace." . File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) keidai.JPG|Precincts File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) zuishinmon.JPG|ZushinmonFile:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) kusu.JPG|Big Camphor (Shizuoka City designated natural monument) File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) torii.JPG|Torii File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) furumiya.JPG|Furumiya === Otorii === [[File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) ichinotorii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Ichino Torii]] The concrete "Ichi-no-Torii" gate, which was built across the city road near Kusanagi Station, had existed since the 1950s, but was removed in 2020. ==== Removal ==== In 2019, it was confirmed that some surface damage had occurred. Since around 2018, Kusanagi Shrine has received inquiries about its management from local residents and parishioners, and when the shrine checked again, it was discovered that the property was not owned by the shrine, and past records of the city and prefecture had to be checked. As a result, in 1975, when Shizuoka Prefecture carried out widening work on the city road as part of the land readjustment project, the prefecture rebuilt the road in the same location to match the new road width as compensation for restoring it to its original condition, but it was once again revealed that the owner was unknown at that time. In other words, it has existed as a local symbol since the 1970s without any maintenance or maintenance. Shizuoka/Kusanagi Shrine approach entrance|date=2020-01-08|publisher=AtS Shizuoka Shimbun SBS}}.Due to the risk of collapse due to deterioration, surrounding residents requested its removal, and Shizuoka City issued a public notice ordering the owner to remove it from March 24, 2020 to June 15 of the same year. We will issue a public notice ordering the owners of the large torii gate and two stone monuments on the South Main Line to remove them! ]}} (Shizuoka City Press Materials, March 24, 2020) Since the owner did not come forward, the city announced a plan to carry out a compulsory summary execution at a cost of 11 million yen, including the cost of removing the two stone monuments next to the torii gate and relocating surrounding telephone and electric cables.{{PDFlink|[https://www.city.shizuoka.lg.jp/000864003.pdf Torii gates, etc. on the South Main Line will be removed by proxy execution]}} (Shizuoka City press material, August 17, 2020), and were removed within the same year.According to research and interviews by Mainichi Shimbun reporters, The evening edition social section of the Tokyo edition of October 31, 2020 (p. 9), articles were found in local newspapers ({{ill|lt=Shizuoka Shimbun|Q904670|WD=Q904670}}) that corroborated that the building was built with funding from many local volunteers, with no direct involvement of the shrine, and that is probably why the ownership and ownership became unclear. A similar and well-known example is the case where the Shiba {{ill|lt=Zojoji Temple #Old Main Gate ``Daimon''|Q249139|WD=Q249139|lt=Zojoji Daimon}} fell out of the capital's property inventory for some reason, making it impossible to confirm its owner. , there is a comment that in religious buildings, the ownership and management are often unclear due to the idea that they belong to everyone, and in this case, it was left unclear when the building was rebuilt in 1975, which led to its demolition. === Setsusha === There are 9 shrines on the south side of the main shrine and 9 shrines on the north side of the shrine. File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) kita-massha.JPG|Kitaside Suesha File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) minami-massha.JPG|South side shrine === Festival === The {{ill|lt=regular festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469}} is held on September 20th, and this day is said to be the day the shrine was founded by Emperor Keiko. On that day, {{ill|lt=fireworks|Q127933|WD=Q127933}}, which evolved from {{ill|lt=fireworks|Q127933|WD=Q127933}}, called "{{ill|lt=dragon force|Q11679206|WD=Q11679206}} (meteor) fireworks" will be launched. These fireworks are said to have continued for the {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259}} years ({{ill|lt=1854|Q7670|WD=Q7670}}-{{ill|lt=1860|Q7691|WD=Q7691}}), and are designated as an intangible folk cultural property by Shizuoka Prefecture. === Cultural assets === === Shizuoka Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * Kusanagi Shrine Ryusei Fireworks - Designated on March 24, 2003. === Cultural property designated by Shizuoka City === *Tangible cultural property ** Fishing lantern (craft) - Dedicated by Morimbu Tayu in {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}}8 ({{ill|lt=1580|Q6712|WD=Q6712}}) at the end of the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845}}. Designated on September 17, 1960. ** Waniguchi (craft) - Dedicated by Morimbu Tayu in 1580 at the end of the Muromachi period. Designated on September 17, 1960. * Natural monument ** Big Camphor - Designated on September 17, 1960. === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320}}{{ill|lt=Shizuoka City|Q174691|WD=Q174691}}{{ill|lt=Shimizu Ward|Q79385|WD=Q79385}}{{ill|lt=Kusanagi (Shizuoka City)|Q11618222|WD=Q11618222|lt=Kusanagi}}349 '''Access''' * From {{ill|lt=Tokai Passenger Railway|Q513679|WD=Q513679}} (JR Tokai) {{ill|lt=Tokaido Line (Shizuoka Area)|Q11527981|WD=Q11527981|lt=Tokaido Main Line}} {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Station (JR Tokai)|Q871079|WD=Q871079|lt=Kusanagi Station}} ** Bus: Take the Shizutetsu Just Line (Miho Kusanagi Line) and get off at the "Kusanagi Jinja-mae" bus stop (about 7 minutes on the bus, a short walk after getting off) '''Surroundings''' * Hyōtanzuka Tumulus (Nishinohara No. 1 Tumulus, {{Wiki coordinates|34|59|53.74|N|138|26|40.83|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|location|name=Hyotanzuka Tumulus}}) - A keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of approximately 45.8 meters {{Sfn|Nishinohara Tumulus Group (Taira)|1979}}. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="Note"}} '''Source''' {{reflist|2}} === References === * Shrine history book * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Meiji Shrine Library|author=|year=1912|chapter=Kusanagi Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Meiji Shrine Library|Q11486103|WD=Q11486103}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Library|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine Library|1912}}}} ** [{{NDLDC|1088244/883}} "Meiji Shrine Journal Materials Fuken Gosha Volume 1"] (National Diet Library Digital Collection) Reference frames 883-884. * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Kenichi Tanikawa|editor-link=Kenichi Tanikawa|author=Kanichi Nomoto|authorlink=Kanichi Nomoto|year=1987|chapter=Kusanagi Shrine|title=Japanese Gods -Shrines and Sacred Sites- 10 Tokai|publisher={{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470}}|isbn=4560022208|ref={{Harvid|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title=Japanese Historical Place Name System 22 Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}|isbn=4582490220|ref=}}** {{Wikicite|reference="Kusanagi Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Nishinohara Tumulus Group"|ref={{Harvid|Nishinohara Tumulus Group (Taira)|1979}}}} ===External link=== [https://kusanagijinjya.jp/ Kusanagi Shrine official website] === Related items === {{Commonscat|Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)}} * {{ill|lt=Amamuragumo Ken|Q14944|WD=Q14944}} (Kusanagi Ken) * {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Shrine|Q11368944|WD=Q11368944}} * {{ill|lt=Yaizu Shrine|Q3571342|WD=Q3571342}} * {{ill|lt=Katori Shrine|Q372380|WD=Q372380}} - {{ill|lt=SMAP|Q1138250|WD=Q1138250}} A shrine with the same name as the member. {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kusanaki Shinsiya}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kusanagi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kusanagi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Kusanagi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suruga Province]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:清水区の歴史]] [[Category:駿河国の式内社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧県社]] [[Category:ヤマトタケル伝説]] [[Category:清水区の建築物]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqq]]

Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}} |Foundation = (tradition) 12th {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}53 |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=September 20th|Q2892|WD=Q2892|1=September 20}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Kusanagi Shrine|coordinates={{coord2|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E}}}} }} {{List of coordinates}} '''Kusanagi Shrine'' (Kusanagi Shrine) is located in {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in (Shizuoka City)|Q11618222|Kusanagi}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} and {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}}. === Enshrined God === The enshrined deity is one of the followingShrine Historysho. . * '''{{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nippon Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}}''' (Yamato Takeru Mikoto) According to the legends of "{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}" and "{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}," during Yamato Takeru's eastern expedition, bandits They tried to set the field where Totakeru was on fire and burn it to death, but Yamatotakeru escaped by mowing down the grass with {{ill|lt=Amamuragumo Sword|Q14944|WD=Q14944|1=Kusanagi no Tsurugi}} and setting fire to it. For this reason, the Ten Murakumo sword is called the ``Kusanagi sword'' {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. To the northeast of our company is {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Shrine|Q11368944|WD=Q11368944|1=Kusanagi Shrine (Q11368944)}} (Yamakiri, Shimizu Ward, Shizuoka City), which is also known as {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} and also called "Kusanagi." However, both shrines are said to be related to the legend of Yamato Takeru's fire disaster, and one theory suggests that this is where the legend of the fire disaster occurred.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}In addition to this place (Kusanagi, Suruga Province), there is a theory that the place where Yamato Takeru's fire disaster is said to have been said is compared to Ono, Sagami Province, and Yaizu, Suruga Province {{Harv|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. . === History === === Founding === [[File:Hyotanzuka-kofun (Shizuoka).JPG|thumb|200px|right|Hyotanzuka Tumulus (Yada, Shimizu Ward)
{{Smaller|The main tomb of the Nishinohara kofun group located at the northern foot of Mt. Udo. Located near Kusanagi Shrine. }}]] According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} in 1953 when the Emperor visited places related to {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nihon Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}}, and the Emperor arrived on September 20th to enshrine the spirit of Nihon Takeru. In addition, the Emperor dedicated the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kusanagi Sword|1=UNKNOWN}} to our company as {{ill|lt=Shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532|1=Shintai}}, and this Kusanagi Sword was discovered in the first year of {{ill|lt=Shudori|Q777413|WD=Q777413|1=Shuchō}} ({{ja It is also said that it was moved to {{ill|lt=Atsuta Shrine|Q482065|WD=Q482065|1=Atsuta Shrine}} by the imperial order of {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenmu|Q335181|WD=Q335181|1=Emperor Tenmu}} in 686|Q30619}}.. Also, according to the company's legend, the company was originally located in a place called ``Tennoubara'' in Kusanagi 1-chome, Shimizu Ward. It is not clear when it was moved to its current location, but one theory is that it was built in {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}18 ({{ill|lt=1590|Q6764|WD=Q6764|1=1590}}).Based on historical research, as mentioned above, the founding of our company is said to have a great deal to do with the legend of Yamato Takeru. In the {{ill|lt=Shizuoka City|Q174691|WD=Q174691|1=Shizuoka (city)}} area, there are large-scale burial mounds such as {{ill|lt=Yatsuyama Tumulus|Q18460128|WD=Q18460128|1=Yatsuyama-kofun}} (total length 110 meters) in {{ill|lt=Aoi Ward|Q1131483|WD=Q1131483|1=Aoi-ku, Shizuoka}} and {{jalink|Shimizu The {{ill|lt=Miikedaira Tumulus|Q107504282|WD=Q107504282|1=Miikedaira-kofun}} (total length 65 meters) in Ku|Q79385}} remains, and it is speculated that the subordination of the powers that built these tombs is behind the legend {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. It is assumed that the chief burial mounds in this area have changed over the course of ``Shimizu-ku Ibara and Yatsuyama Hills (early 4th century) → Sena Hills (middle 5th century) → North foot of Mt. Udo (late 6th century)'' {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods) |1987}}{{Sfn|Nishinohara Tumulus Group (Taira)|1979}} Among these, {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Shrine|Q11368944|WD=Q11368944|1=Kusanagi Shrine (Q11368944)}} is located near Ibara in Shimizu-ku, and our company is located at the northern foot of Mt. Udo. There is also a theory that the name ``Kusanagi'' comes from a place name related to slash-and-burn cultivation {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. It has also been pointed out that the legend of Yamato Takeru lighting fires is a well-known method for preventing the spread of wildfires, and it is assumed that the legend of Yamato Takeru's fire disaster was established based on the preceding slash-and-burn place names {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. Before the legend of Yamato Takeru was established, it is believed that the shrine was the god of the mountains and the god of slash-and-burn crops, or the god of water since it was located in a valley {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. === Outline === {{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=In the Shinmeicho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, ``Kusanagi Shrine'' is written in {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|WD=Q864170|1=Suruga Province}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Arido District|1=UNKNOWN}}, and it is listed as {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}}{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. It was worshiped by samurai families due to its connection with the Yamato Takeru tradition, and was worshiped for {{ill|lt=Tenkei|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340|1=Tengyō}} years ({{ill|lt=877 years|Q23905|WD=Q23905|1=877}}-{{ill|lt=885 years|Q24894|WD=Q24894|1=885}}) says that an offering was made to pacify the {{ill|lt=Johei Tenkyo Rebellion#Taira no Masakado Rebellion|Q2975322|WD=Q2975322|lt=Taira no Masakado Rebellion|1=Tengyō no Ran}} {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}.In the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|WD=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}}, the shogun {{ill|lt=Eikyo|Q1193059|WD=Q1193059|1=Eikyō}}4 ({{ill|lt=1432|Q6349|WD=Q6349|1=1432}}) {{ill|lt=Yoshitaka|Q11464813|WD=Q11464813|1=Gyōkō}}, who followed |Ashikaga Yoshinori|Q239170}} to Suruga Shimoki, wrote about Kusanagi Shrine {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. In {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}8 ({{ill|lt=1580|Q6712|WD=Q6712|1=1580}}), a fishing lantern and a crocodile mouth (both cultural properties designated by Shizuoka City) were dedicated to the shrine by the petitioner, Moriminbu Dayu.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. In the 10th year of Tensho ({{ill|lt=1582|Q6719|WD=Q6719|1=1582}}), felling of bamboo and trees was prohibited, and in the 18th year of Tensho ({{ill|lt=1590|Q6764|WD=Q6764|1=1590}}), 18 koku of Kusanagi-go was granted as shrine territory by Daisuke Minbu, a social worker {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. This shrine territory was also relieved by Akira Yokotamura in {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}4 ({{ill|lt=1599|Q6803|WD=Q6803|1=1599}}) {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. The shrine territory ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shuinji|1=UNKNOWN}}) in the Edo period was 50 koku {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}.After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}3 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), in {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} It was ranked as {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} and was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} on July 24, 1879|1912}}. === God level === *Shogoi Lower Rank (``Suruga Province Shinto Name Book'') - Written as ``Kusanagi Tenjin.'' === Precincts === The shrine grounds are located in the valley of the upper reaches of the Kusanagi River at the northern foot of Mt. Udo.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. The area is 0.7 hectares. The trunk of the camphor tree that stands within the shrine grounds has withered and only the outer skin remains, but it is an old tree that is 6 meters high and 25 meters in circumference and is said to be over 1,000 years old, and has been designated as a natural monument by Shizuoka City. The flat land near {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Station (JR Tokai)|Q871079|WD=Q871079|lt=Kusanagi Station|1=Kusanagi Station (JR Central)}} is called "Tennoubara" . It is said that Kusanagi Shrine was once located on this land of Emperor Hara, and the place is still known as the "Old Palace." . File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) keidai.JPG|Precincts File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) zuishinmon.JPG|ZushinmonFile:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) kusu.JPG|Big Camphor (Shizuoka City designated natural monument) File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) torii.JPG|Torii File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) furumiya.JPG|Furumiya === Otorii === [[File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) ichinotorii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Ichino Torii]] The concrete "Ichi-no-Torii" gate, which was built across the city road near Kusanagi Station, had existed since the 1950s, but was removed in 2020. ==== Removal ==== In 2019, it was confirmed that some surface damage had occurred. Since around 2018, Kusanagi Shrine has received inquiries about its management from local residents and parishioners, and when the shrine checked again, it was discovered that the property was not owned by the shrine, and past records of the city and prefecture had to be checked. As a result, in 1975, when Shizuoka Prefecture carried out widening work on the city road as part of the land readjustment project, the prefecture rebuilt the road in the same location to match the new road width as compensation for restoring it to its original condition, but it was once again revealed that the owner was unknown at that time. In other words, it has existed as a local symbol since the 1970s without any maintenance or maintenance. Shizuoka/Kusanagi Shrine approach entrance|date=2020-01-08|publisher=AtS Shizuoka Shimbun SBS}}.Due to the risk of collapse due to deterioration, surrounding residents requested its removal, and Shizuoka City issued a public notice ordering the owner to remove it from March 24, 2020 to June 15 of the same year. We will issue a public notice ordering the owners of the large torii gate and two stone monuments on the South Main Line to remove them! ]}} (Shizuoka City Press Materials, March 24, 2020) Since the owner did not come forward, the city announced a plan to carry out a compulsory summary execution at a cost of 11 million yen, including the cost of removing the two stone monuments next to the torii gate and relocating surrounding telephone and electric cables.{{PDFlink|[https://www.city.shizuoka.lg.jp/000864003.pdf Torii gates, etc. on the South Main Line will be removed by proxy execution]}} (Shizuoka City press material, August 17, 2020), and were removed within the same year.According to research and interviews by Mainichi Shimbun reporters, The evening edition social section of the Tokyo edition of October 31, 2020 (p. 9), articles were found in local newspapers ({{ill|lt=Shizuoka Shimbun|Q904670|WD=Q904670|1=Shizuoka Shimbun}}) that corroborated that the building was built with funding from many local volunteers, with no direct involvement of the shrine, and that is probably why the ownership and ownership became unclear. A similar and well-known example is the case where the Shiba {{ill|lt=Zojoji Temple #Old Main Gate ``Daimon''|Q249139|WD=Q249139|lt=Zojoji Daimon|1=Zōjō-ji}} fell out of the capital's property inventory for some reason, making it impossible to confirm its owner. , there is a comment that in religious buildings, the ownership and management are often unclear due to the idea that they belong to everyone, and in this case, it was left unclear when the building was rebuilt in 1975, which led to its demolition. === Setsusha === There are 9 shrines on the south side of the main shrine and 9 shrines on the north side of the shrine. File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) kita-massha.JPG|Kitaside Suesha File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) minami-massha.JPG|South side shrine === Festival === The {{ill|lt=regular festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469|1=Reisai}} is held on September 20th, and this day is said to be the day the shrine was founded by Emperor Keiko. On that day, {{ill|lt=fireworks|Q127933|WD=Q127933|1=Fireworks}}, which evolved from {{ill|lt=fireworks|Q127933|WD=Q127933|1=Fireworks}}, called "{{ill|lt=dragon force|Q11679206|WD=Q11679206|1=Ryūsei (signal rocket)}} (meteor) fireworks" will be launched. These fireworks are said to have continued for the {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259|1=Ansei}} years ({{ill|lt=1854|Q7670|WD=Q7670|1=1854}}-{{ill|lt=1860|Q7691|WD=Q7691|1=1860}}), and are designated as an intangible folk cultural property by Shizuoka Prefecture. === Cultural assets === === Shizuoka Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * Kusanagi Shrine Ryusei Fireworks - Designated on March 24, 2003. === Cultural property designated by Shizuoka City === *Tangible cultural property ** Fishing lantern (craft) - Dedicated by Morimbu Tayu in {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}8 ({{ill|lt=1580|Q6712|WD=Q6712|1=1580}}) at the end of the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845|1=Muromachi period}}. Designated on September 17, 1960. ** Waniguchi (craft) - Dedicated by Morimbu Tayu in 1580 at the end of the Muromachi period. Designated on September 17, 1960. * Natural monument ** Big Camphor - Designated on September 17, 1960. === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Shizuoka City|Q174691|WD=Q174691|1=Shizuoka (city)}}{{ill|lt=Shimizu Ward|Q79385|WD=Q79385|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}}{{ill|lt=Kusanagi (Shizuoka City)|Q11618222|WD=Q11618222|lt=Kusanagi|1=Kusanagi}}349 '''Access''' * From {{ill|lt=Tokai Passenger Railway|Q513679|WD=Q513679|1=Central Japan Railway Company}} (JR Tokai) {{ill|lt=Tokaido Line (Shizuoka Area)|Q11527981|WD=Q11527981|lt=Tokaido Main Line|1=Tōkaidō Line (Shizuoka area)}} {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Station (JR Tokai)|Q871079|WD=Q871079|lt=Kusanagi Station|1=Kusanagi Station (JR Central)}} ** Bus: Take the Shizutetsu Just Line (Miho Kusanagi Line) and get off at the "Kusanagi Jinja-mae" bus stop (about 7 minutes on the bus, a short walk after getting off) '''Surroundings''' * Hyōtanzuka Tumulus (Nishinohara No. 1 Tumulus, {{Wiki coordinates|34|59|53.74|N|138|26|40.83|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|location|name=Hyotanzuka Tumulus}}) - A keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of approximately 45.8 meters {{Sfn|Nishinohara Tumulus Group (Taira)|1979}}. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="Note"}} '''Source''' {{reflist|2}} === References === * Shrine history book * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Meiji Shrine Library|author=|year=1912|chapter=Kusanagi Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Meiji Shrine Library|Q11486103|WD=Q11486103|1=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Library|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine Library|1912}}}} ** [{{NDLDC|1088244/883}} "Meiji Shrine Journal Materials Fuken Gosha Volume 1"] (National Diet Library Digital Collection) Reference frames 883-884. * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Kenichi Tanikawa|editor-link=Kenichi Tanikawa|author=Kanichi Nomoto|authorlink=Kanichi Nomoto|year=1987|chapter=Kusanagi Shrine|title=Japanese Gods -Shrines and Sacred Sites- 10 Tokai|publisher={{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}|isbn=4560022208|ref={{Harvid|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title=Japanese Historical Place Name System 22 Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490220|ref=}}** {{Wikicite|reference="Kusanagi Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Nishinohara Tumulus Group"|ref={{Harvid|Nishinohara Tumulus Group (Taira)|1979}}}} ===External link=== [https://kusanagijinjya.jp/ Kusanagi Shrine official website] === Related items === {{Commonscat|Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)}} * {{ill|lt=Amamuragumo Ken|Q14944|WD=Q14944|1=Kusanagi no Tsurugi}} (Kusanagi Ken) * {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Shrine|Q11368944|WD=Q11368944|1=Kusanagi Shrine (Q11368944)}} * {{ill|lt=Yaizu Shrine|Q3571342|WD=Q3571342|1=Yaizu Shrine}} * {{ill|lt=Katori Shrine|Q372380|WD=Q372380|1=Katori Jingū}} - Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}} |Foundation = (tradition) 12th {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}53 |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=September 20th|Q2892|WD=Q2892|1=September 20}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Kusanagi Shrine|coordinates={{coord2|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E}}}} }} {{List of coordinates}} '''Kusanagi Shrine'' (Kusanagi Shrine) is located in {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in (Shizuoka City)|Q11618222|Kusanagi}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} and {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}}. === Enshrined God === The enshrined deity is one of the followingShrine Historysho. . * '''{{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nippon Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}}''' (Yamato Takeru Mikoto) According to the legends of "{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}" and "{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}," during Yamato Takeru's eastern expedition, bandits They tried to set the field where Totakeru was on fire and burn it to death, but Yamatotakeru escaped by mowing down the grass with {{ill|lt=Amamuragumo Sword|Q14944|WD=Q14944|1=Kusanagi no Tsurugi}} and setting fire to it. For this reason, the Ten Murakumo sword is called the ``Kusanagi sword'' {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. To the northeast of our company is {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Shrine|Q11368944|WD=Q11368944|1=Kusanagi Shrine (Q11368944)}} (Yamakiri, Shimizu Ward, Shizuoka City), which is also known as {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} and also called "Kusanagi." However, both shrines are said to be related to the legend of Yamato Takeru's fire disaster, and one theory suggests that this is where the legend of the fire disaster occurred.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}In addition to this place (Kusanagi, Suruga Province), there is a theory that the place where Yamato Takeru's fire disaster is said to have been said is compared to Ono, Sagami Province, and Yaizu, Suruga Province {{Harv|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. . === History === === Founding === [[File:Hyotanzuka-kofun (Shizuoka).JPG|thumb|200px|right|Hyotanzuka Tumulus (Yada, Shimizu Ward)
{{Smaller|The main tomb of the Nishinohara kofun group located at the northern foot of Mt. Udo. Located near Kusanagi Shrine. }}]] According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} in 1953 when the Emperor visited places related to {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nihon Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}}, and the Emperor arrived on September 20th to enshrine the spirit of Nihon Takeru. In addition, the Emperor dedicated the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kusanagi Sword|1=UNKNOWN}} to our company as {{ill|lt=Shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532|1=Shintai}}, and this Kusanagi Sword was discovered in the first year of {{ill|lt=Shudori|Q777413|WD=Q777413|1=Shuchō}} ({{ja It is also said that it was moved to {{ill|lt=Atsuta Shrine|Q482065|WD=Q482065|1=Atsuta Shrine}} by the imperial order of {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenmu|Q335181|WD=Q335181|1=Emperor Tenmu}} in 686|Q30619}}.. Also, according to the company's legend, the company was originally located in a place called ``Tennoubara'' in Kusanagi 1-chome, Shimizu Ward. It is not clear when it was moved to its current location, but one theory is that it was built in {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}18 ({{ill|lt=1590|Q6764|WD=Q6764|1=1590}}).Based on historical research, as mentioned above, the founding of our company is said to have a great deal to do with the legend of Yamato Takeru. In the {{ill|lt=Shizuoka City|Q174691|WD=Q174691|1=Shizuoka (city)}} area, there are large-scale burial mounds such as {{ill|lt=Yatsuyama Tumulus|Q18460128|WD=Q18460128|1=Yatsuyama-kofun}} (total length 110 meters) in {{ill|lt=Aoi Ward|Q1131483|WD=Q1131483|1=Aoi-ku, Shizuoka}} and {{jalink|Shimizu The {{ill|lt=Miikedaira Tumulus|Q107504282|WD=Q107504282|1=Miikedaira-kofun}} (total length 65 meters) in Ku|Q79385}} remains, and it is speculated that the subordination of the powers that built these tombs is behind the legend {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. It is assumed that the chief burial mounds in this area have changed over the course of ``Shimizu-ku Ibara and Yatsuyama Hills (early 4th century) → Sena Hills (middle 5th century) → North foot of Mt. Udo (late 6th century)'' {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods) |1987}}{{Sfn|Nishinohara Tumulus Group (Taira)|1979}} Among these, {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Shrine|Q11368944|WD=Q11368944|1=Kusanagi Shrine (Q11368944)}} is located near Ibara in Shimizu-ku, and our company is located at the northern foot of Mt. Udo. There is also a theory that the name ``Kusanagi'' comes from a place name related to slash-and-burn cultivation {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. It has also been pointed out that the legend of Yamato Takeru lighting fires is a well-known method for preventing the spread of wildfires, and it is assumed that the legend of Yamato Takeru's fire disaster was established based on the preceding slash-and-burn place names {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. Before the legend of Yamato Takeru was established, it is believed that the shrine was the god of the mountains and the god of slash-and-burn crops, or the god of water since it was located in a valley {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. === Outline === {{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=In the Shinmeicho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, ``Kusanagi Shrine'' is written in {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|WD=Q864170|1=Suruga Province}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Arido District|1=UNKNOWN}}, and it is listed as {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}}{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. It was worshiped by samurai families due to its connection with the Yamato Takeru tradition, and was worshiped for {{ill|lt=Tenkei|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340|1=Tengyō}} years ({{ill|lt=877 years|Q23905|WD=Q23905|1=877}}-{{ill|lt=885 years|Q24894|WD=Q24894|1=885}}) says that an offering was made to pacify the {{ill|lt=Johei Tenkyo Rebellion#Taira no Masakado Rebellion|Q2975322|WD=Q2975322|lt=Taira no Masakado Rebellion|1=Tengyō no Ran}} {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}.In the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|WD=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}}, the shogun {{ill|lt=Eikyo|Q1193059|WD=Q1193059|1=Eikyō}}4 ({{ill|lt=1432|Q6349|WD=Q6349|1=1432}}) {{ill|lt=Yoshitaka|Q11464813|WD=Q11464813|1=Gyōkō}}, who followed |Ashikaga Yoshinori|Q239170}} to Suruga Shimoki, wrote about Kusanagi Shrine {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. In {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}8 ({{ill|lt=1580|Q6712|WD=Q6712|1=1580}}), a fishing lantern and a crocodile mouth (both cultural properties designated by Shizuoka City) were dedicated to the shrine by the petitioner, Moriminbu Dayu.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. In the 10th year of Tensho ({{ill|lt=1582|Q6719|WD=Q6719|1=1582}}), felling of bamboo and trees was prohibited, and in the 18th year of Tensho ({{ill|lt=1590|Q6764|WD=Q6764|1=1590}}), 18 koku of Kusanagi-go was granted as shrine territory by Daisuke Minbu, a social worker {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. This shrine territory was also relieved by Akira Yokotamura in {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}4 ({{ill|lt=1599|Q6803|WD=Q6803|1=1599}}) {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. The shrine territory ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shuinji|1=UNKNOWN}}) in the Edo period was 50 koku {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}.After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}3 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), in {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} It was ranked as {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} and was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} on July 24, 1879|1912}}. === God level === *Shogoi Lower Rank (``Suruga Province Shinto Name Book'') - Written as ``Kusanagi Tenjin.'' === Precincts === The shrine grounds are located in the valley of the upper reaches of the Kusanagi River at the northern foot of Mt. Udo.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. The area is 0.7 hectares. The trunk of the camphor tree that stands within the shrine grounds has withered and only the outer skin remains, but it is an old tree that is 6 meters high and 25 meters in circumference and is said to be over 1,000 years old, and has been designated as a natural monument by Shizuoka City. The flat land near {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Station (JR Tokai)|Q871079|WD=Q871079|lt=Kusanagi Station|1=Kusanagi Station (JR Central)}} is called "Tennoubara" . It is said that Kusanagi Shrine was once located on this land of Emperor Hara, and the place is still known as the "Old Palace." . File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) keidai.JPG|Precincts File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) zuishinmon.JPG|ZushinmonFile:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) kusu.JPG|Big Camphor (Shizuoka City designated natural monument) File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) torii.JPG|Torii File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) furumiya.JPG|Furumiya === Otorii === [[File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) ichinotorii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Ichino Torii]] The concrete "Ichi-no-Torii" gate, which was built across the city road near Kusanagi Station, had existed since the 1950s, but was removed in 2020. ==== Removal ==== In 2019, it was confirmed that some surface damage had occurred. Since around 2018, Kusanagi Shrine has received inquiries about its management from local residents and parishioners, and when the shrine checked again, it was discovered that the property was not owned by the shrine, and past records of the city and prefecture had to be checked. As a result, in 1975, when Shizuoka Prefecture carried out widening work on the city road as part of the land readjustment project, the prefecture rebuilt the road in the same location to match the new road width as compensation for restoring it to its original condition, but it was once again revealed that the owner was unknown at that time. In other words, it has existed as a local symbol since the 1970s without any maintenance or maintenance. Shizuoka/Kusanagi Shrine approach entrance|date=2020-01-08|publisher=AtS Shizuoka Shimbun SBS}}.Due to the risk of collapse due to deterioration, surrounding residents requested its removal, and Shizuoka City issued a public notice ordering the owner to remove it from March 24, 2020 to June 15 of the same year. We will issue a public notice ordering the owners of the large torii gate and two stone monuments on the South Main Line to remove them! ]}} (Shizuoka City Press Materials, March 24, 2020) Since the owner did not come forward, the city announced a plan to carry out a compulsory summary execution at a cost of 11 million yen, including the cost of removing the two stone monuments next to the torii gate and relocating surrounding telephone and electric cables.{{PDFlink|[https://www.city.shizuoka.lg.jp/000864003.pdf Torii gates, etc. on the South Main Line will be removed by proxy execution]}} (Shizuoka City press material, August 17, 2020), and were removed within the same year.According to research and interviews by Mainichi Shimbun reporters, The evening edition social section of the Tokyo edition of October 31, 2020 (p. 9), articles were found in local newspapers ({{ill|lt=Shizuoka Shimbun|Q904670|WD=Q904670|1=Shizuoka Shimbun}}) that corroborated that the building was built with funding from many local volunteers, with no direct involvement of the shrine, and that is probably why the ownership and ownership became unclear. A similar and well-known example is the case where the Shiba {{ill|lt=Zojoji Temple #Old Main Gate ``Daimon''|Q249139|WD=Q249139|lt=Zojoji Daimon|1=Zōjō-ji}} fell out of the capital's property inventory for some reason, making it impossible to confirm its owner. , there is a comment that in religious buildings, the ownership and management are often unclear due to the idea that they belong to everyone, and in this case, it was left unclear when the building was rebuilt in 1975, which led to its demolition. === Setsusha === There are 9 shrines on the south side of the main shrine and 9 shrines on the north side of the shrine. File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) kita-massha.JPG|Kitaside Suesha File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) minami-massha.JPG|South side shrine === Festival === The {{ill|lt=regular festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469|1=Reisai}} is held on September 20th, and this day is said to be the day the shrine was founded by Emperor Keiko. On that day, {{ill|lt=fireworks|Q127933|WD=Q127933|1=Fireworks}}, which evolved from {{ill|lt=fireworks|Q127933|WD=Q127933|1=Fireworks}}, called "{{ill|lt=dragon force|Q11679206|WD=Q11679206|1=Ryūsei (signal rocket)}} (meteor) fireworks" will be launched. These fireworks are said to have continued for the {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259|1=Ansei}} years ({{ill|lt=1854|Q7670|WD=Q7670|1=1854}}-{{ill|lt=1860|Q7691|WD=Q7691|1=1860}}), and are designated as an intangible folk cultural property by Shizuoka Prefecture. === Cultural assets === === Shizuoka Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * Kusanagi Shrine Ryusei Fireworks - Designated on March 24, 2003. === Cultural property designated by Shizuoka City === *Tangible cultural property ** Fishing lantern (craft) - Dedicated by Morimbu Tayu in {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}8 ({{ill|lt=1580|Q6712|WD=Q6712|1=1580}}) at the end of the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845|1=Muromachi period}}. Designated on September 17, 1960. ** Waniguchi (craft) - Dedicated by Morimbu Tayu in 1580 at the end of the Muromachi period. Designated on September 17, 1960. * Natural monument ** Big Camphor - Designated on September 17, 1960. === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Shizuoka City|Q174691|WD=Q174691|1=Shizuoka (city)}}{{ill|lt=Shimizu Ward|Q79385|WD=Q79385|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}}{{ill|lt=Kusanagi (Shizuoka City)|Q11618222|WD=Q11618222|lt=Kusanagi|1=Kusanagi}}349 '''Access''' * From {{ill|lt=Tokai Passenger Railway|Q513679|WD=Q513679|1=Central Japan Railway Company}} (JR Tokai) {{ill|lt=Tokaido Line (Shizuoka Area)|Q11527981|WD=Q11527981|lt=Tokaido Main Line|1=Tōkaidō Line (Shizuoka area)}} {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Station (JR Tokai)|Q871079|WD=Q871079|lt=Kusanagi Station|1=Kusanagi Station (JR Central)}} ** Bus: Take the Shizutetsu Just Line (Miho Kusanagi Line) and get off at the "Kusanagi Jinja-mae" bus stop (about 7 minutes on the bus, a short walk after getting off) '''Surroundings''' * Hyōtanzuka Tumulus (Nishinohara No. 1 Tumulus, {{Wiki coordinates|34|59|53.74|N|138|26|40.83|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|location|name=Hyotanzuka Tumulus}}) - A keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of approximately 45.8 meters {{Sfn|Nishinohara Tumulus Group (Taira)|1979}}. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="Note"}} '''Source''' {{reflist|2}} === References === * Shrine history book * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Meiji Shrine Library|author=|year=1912|chapter=Kusanagi Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Meiji Shrine Library|Q11486103|WD=Q11486103|1=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Library|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine Library|1912}}}} ** [{{NDLDC|1088244/883}} "Meiji Shrine Journal Materials Fuken Gosha Volume 1"] (National Diet Library Digital Collection) Reference frames 883-884. * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Kenichi Tanikawa|editor-link=Kenichi Tanikawa|author=Kanichi Nomoto|authorlink=Kanichi Nomoto|year=1987|chapter=Kusanagi Shrine|title=Japanese Gods -Shrines and Sacred Sites- 10 Tokai|publisher={{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}|isbn=4560022208|ref={{Harvid|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title=Japanese Historical Place Name System 22 Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490220|ref=}}** {{Wikicite|reference="Kusanagi Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Nishinohara Tumulus Group"|ref={{Harvid|Nishinohara Tumulus Group (Taira)|1979}}}} ===External link=== [https://kusanagijinjya.jp/ Kusanagi Shrine official website] === Related items === {{Commonscat|Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)}} * {{ill|lt=Amamuragumo Ken|Q14944|WD=Q14944|1=Kusanagi no Tsurugi}} (Kusanagi Ken) * {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Shrine|Q11368944|WD=Q11368944|1=Kusanagi Shrine (Q11368944)}} * {{ill|lt=Yaizu Shrine|Q3571342|WD=Q3571342|1=Yaizu Shrine}} * {{ill|lt=Katori Shrine|Q372380|WD=Q372380|1=Katori Jingū}} - Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}} |Foundation = (tradition) 12th {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}53 |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=September 20th|Q2892|WD=Q2892|1=September 20}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Kusanagi Shrine|coordinates={{coord2|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|59|40.1|N|138|27|10.1|E}}}} }} {{List of coordinates}} '''Kusanagi Shrine'' (Kusanagi Shrine) is located in {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in (Shizuoka City)|Q11618222|Kusanagi}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} and {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}}. === Enshrined God === The enshrined deity is one of the followingShrine Historysho. . * '''{{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nippon Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}}''' (Yamato Takeru Mikoto) According to the legends of "{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}" and "{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}," during Yamato Takeru's eastern expedition, bandits They tried to set the field where Totakeru was on fire and burn it to death, but Yamatotakeru escaped by mowing down the grass with {{ill|lt=Amamuragumo Sword|Q14944|WD=Q14944|1=Kusanagi no Tsurugi}} and setting fire to it. For this reason, the Ten Murakumo sword is called the ``Kusanagi sword'' {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. To the northeast of our company is {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Shrine|Q11368944|WD=Q11368944|1=Kusanagi Shrine (Q11368944)}} (Yamakiri, Shimizu Ward, Shizuoka City), which is also known as {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} and also called "Kusanagi." However, both shrines are said to be related to the legend of Yamato Takeru's fire disaster, and one theory suggests that this is where the legend of the fire disaster occurred.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}In addition to this place (Kusanagi, Suruga Province), there is a theory that the place where Yamato Takeru's fire disaster is said to have been said is compared to Ono, Sagami Province, and Yaizu, Suruga Province {{Harv|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. . === History === === Founding === [[File:Hyotanzuka-kofun (Shizuoka).JPG|thumb|200px|right|Hyotanzuka Tumulus (Yada, Shimizu Ward)
{{Smaller|The main tomb of the Nishinohara kofun group located at the northern foot of Mt. Udo. Located near Kusanagi Shrine. }}]] According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} in 1953 when the Emperor visited places related to {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nihon Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}}, and the Emperor arrived on September 20th to enshrine the spirit of Nihon Takeru. In addition, the Emperor dedicated the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kusanagi Sword|1=UNKNOWN}} to our company as {{ill|lt=Shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532|1=Shintai}}, and this Kusanagi Sword was discovered in the first year of {{ill|lt=Shudori|Q777413|WD=Q777413|1=Shuchō}} ({{ja It is also said that it was moved to {{ill|lt=Atsuta Shrine|Q482065|WD=Q482065|1=Atsuta Shrine}} by the imperial order of {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenmu|Q335181|WD=Q335181|1=Emperor Tenmu}} in 686|Q30619}}.. Also, according to the company's legend, the company was originally located in a place called ``Tennoubara'' in Kusanagi 1-chome, Shimizu Ward. It is not clear when it was moved to its current location, but one theory is that it was built in {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}18 ({{ill|lt=1590|Q6764|WD=Q6764|1=1590}}).Based on historical research, as mentioned above, the founding of our company is said to have a great deal to do with the legend of Yamato Takeru. In the {{ill|lt=Shizuoka City|Q174691|WD=Q174691|1=Shizuoka (city)}} area, there are large-scale burial mounds such as {{ill|lt=Yatsuyama Tumulus|Q18460128|WD=Q18460128|1=Yatsuyama-kofun}} (total length 110 meters) in {{ill|lt=Aoi Ward|Q1131483|WD=Q1131483|1=Aoi-ku, Shizuoka}} and {{jalink|Shimizu The {{ill|lt=Miikedaira Tumulus|Q107504282|WD=Q107504282|1=Miikedaira-kofun}} (total length 65 meters) in Ku|Q79385}} remains, and it is speculated that the subordination of the powers that built these tombs is behind the legend {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. It is assumed that the chief burial mounds in this area have changed over the course of ``Shimizu-ku Ibara and Yatsuyama Hills (early 4th century) → Sena Hills (middle 5th century) → North foot of Mt. Udo (late 6th century)'' {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods) |1987}}{{Sfn|Nishinohara Tumulus Group (Taira)|1979}} Among these, {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Shrine|Q11368944|WD=Q11368944|1=Kusanagi Shrine (Q11368944)}} is located near Ibara in Shimizu-ku, and our company is located at the northern foot of Mt. Udo. There is also a theory that the name ``Kusanagi'' comes from a place name related to slash-and-burn cultivation {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. It has also been pointed out that the legend of Yamato Takeru lighting fires is a well-known method for preventing the spread of wildfires, and it is assumed that the legend of Yamato Takeru's fire disaster was established based on the preceding slash-and-burn place names {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. Before the legend of Yamato Takeru was established, it is believed that the shrine was the god of the mountains and the god of slash-and-burn crops, or the god of water since it was located in a valley {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. === Outline === {{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=In the Shinmeicho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, ``Kusanagi Shrine'' is written in {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|WD=Q864170|1=Suruga Province}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Arido District|1=UNKNOWN}}, and it is listed as {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}}{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. It was worshiped by samurai families due to its connection with the Yamato Takeru tradition, and was worshiped for {{ill|lt=Tenkei|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340|1=Tengyō}} years ({{ill|lt=877 years|Q23905|WD=Q23905|1=877}}-{{ill|lt=885 years|Q24894|WD=Q24894|1=885}}) says that an offering was made to pacify the {{ill|lt=Johei Tenkyo Rebellion#Taira no Masakado Rebellion|Q2975322|WD=Q2975322|lt=Taira no Masakado Rebellion|1=Tengyō no Ran}} {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}.In the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|WD=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}}, the shogun {{ill|lt=Eikyo|Q1193059|WD=Q1193059|1=Eikyō}}4 ({{ill|lt=1432|Q6349|WD=Q6349|1=1432}}) {{ill|lt=Yoshitaka|Q11464813|WD=Q11464813|1=Gyōkō}}, who followed |Ashikaga Yoshinori|Q239170}} to Suruga Shimoki, wrote about Kusanagi Shrine {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. In {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}8 ({{ill|lt=1580|Q6712|WD=Q6712|1=1580}}), a fishing lantern and a crocodile mouth (both cultural properties designated by Shizuoka City) were dedicated to the shrine by the petitioner, Moriminbu Dayu.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. In the 10th year of Tensho ({{ill|lt=1582|Q6719|WD=Q6719|1=1582}}), felling of bamboo and trees was prohibited, and in the 18th year of Tensho ({{ill|lt=1590|Q6764|WD=Q6764|1=1590}}), 18 koku of Kusanagi-go was granted as shrine territory by Daisuke Minbu, a social worker {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. This shrine territory was also relieved by Akira Yokotamura in {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}4 ({{ill|lt=1599|Q6803|WD=Q6803|1=1599}}) {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}. The shrine territory ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shuinji|1=UNKNOWN}}) in the Edo period was 50 koku {{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}.After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}3 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), in {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} It was ranked as {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} and was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} on July 24, 1879|1912}}. === God level === *Shogoi Lower Rank (``Suruga Province Shinto Name Book'') - Written as ``Kusanagi Tenjin.'' === Precincts === The shrine grounds are located in the valley of the upper reaches of the Kusanagi River at the northern foot of Mt. Udo.{{Sfn|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. The area is 0.7 hectares. The trunk of the camphor tree that stands within the shrine grounds has withered and only the outer skin remains, but it is an old tree that is 6 meters high and 25 meters in circumference and is said to be over 1,000 years old, and has been designated as a natural monument by Shizuoka City. The flat land near {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Station (JR Tokai)|Q871079|WD=Q871079|lt=Kusanagi Station|1=Kusanagi Station (JR Central)}} is called "Tennoubara" . It is said that Kusanagi Shrine was once located on this land of Emperor Hara, and the place is still known as the "Old Palace." . File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) keidai.JPG|Precincts File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) zuishinmon.JPG|ZushinmonFile:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) kusu.JPG|Big Camphor (Shizuoka City designated natural monument) File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) torii.JPG|Torii File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) furumiya.JPG|Furumiya === Otorii === [[File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) ichinotorii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Ichino Torii]] The concrete "Ichi-no-Torii" gate, which was built across the city road near Kusanagi Station, had existed since the 1950s, but was removed in 2020. ==== Removal ==== In 2019, it was confirmed that some surface damage had occurred. Since around 2018, Kusanagi Shrine has received inquiries about its management from local residents and parishioners, and when the shrine checked again, it was discovered that the property was not owned by the shrine, and past records of the city and prefecture had to be checked. As a result, in 1975, when Shizuoka Prefecture carried out widening work on the city road as part of the land readjustment project, the prefecture rebuilt the road in the same location to match the new road width as compensation for restoring it to its original condition, but it was once again revealed that the owner was unknown at that time. In other words, it has existed as a local symbol since the 1970s without any maintenance or maintenance. Shizuoka/Kusanagi Shrine approach entrance|date=2020-01-08|publisher=AtS Shizuoka Shimbun SBS}}.Due to the risk of collapse due to deterioration, surrounding residents requested its removal, and Shizuoka City issued a public notice ordering the owner to remove it from March 24, 2020 to June 15 of the same year. We will issue a public notice ordering the owners of the large torii gate and two stone monuments on the South Main Line to remove them! ]}} (Shizuoka City Press Materials, March 24, 2020) Since the owner did not come forward, the city announced a plan to carry out a compulsory summary execution at a cost of 11 million yen, including the cost of removing the two stone monuments next to the torii gate and relocating surrounding telephone and electric cables.{{PDFlink|[https://www.city.shizuoka.lg.jp/000864003.pdf Torii gates, etc. on the South Main Line will be removed by proxy execution]}} (Shizuoka City press material, August 17, 2020), and were removed within the same year.According to research and interviews by Mainichi Shimbun reporters, The evening edition social section of the Tokyo edition of October 31, 2020 (p. 9), articles were found in local newspapers ({{ill|lt=Shizuoka Shimbun|Q904670|WD=Q904670|1=Shizuoka Shimbun}}) that corroborated that the building was built with funding from many local volunteers, with no direct involvement of the shrine, and that is probably why the ownership and ownership became unclear. A similar and well-known example is the case where the Shiba {{ill|lt=Zojoji Temple #Old Main Gate ``Daimon''|Q249139|WD=Q249139|lt=Zojoji Daimon|1=Zōjō-ji}} fell out of the capital's property inventory for some reason, making it impossible to confirm its owner. , there is a comment that in religious buildings, the ownership and management are often unclear due to the idea that they belong to everyone, and in this case, it was left unclear when the building was rebuilt in 1975, which led to its demolition. === Setsusha === There are 9 shrines on the south side of the main shrine and 9 shrines on the north side of the shrine. File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) kita-massha.JPG|Kitaside Suesha File:Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka) minami-massha.JPG|South side shrine === Festival === The {{ill|lt=regular festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469|1=Reisai}} is held on September 20th, and this day is said to be the day the shrine was founded by Emperor Keiko. On that day, {{ill|lt=fireworks|Q127933|WD=Q127933|1=Fireworks}}, which evolved from {{ill|lt=fireworks|Q127933|WD=Q127933|1=Fireworks}}, called "{{ill|lt=dragon force|Q11679206|WD=Q11679206|1=Ryūsei (signal rocket)}} (meteor) fireworks" will be launched. These fireworks are said to have continued for the {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259|1=Ansei}} years ({{ill|lt=1854|Q7670|WD=Q7670|1=1854}}-{{ill|lt=1860|Q7691|WD=Q7691|1=1860}}), and are designated as an intangible folk cultural property by Shizuoka Prefecture. === Cultural assets === === Shizuoka Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * Kusanagi Shrine Ryusei Fireworks - Designated on March 24, 2003. === Cultural property designated by Shizuoka City === *Tangible cultural property ** Fishing lantern (craft) - Dedicated by Morimbu Tayu in {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}8 ({{ill|lt=1580|Q6712|WD=Q6712|1=1580}}) at the end of the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845|1=Muromachi period}}. Designated on September 17, 1960. ** Waniguchi (craft) - Dedicated by Morimbu Tayu in 1580 at the end of the Muromachi period. Designated on September 17, 1960. * Natural monument ** Big Camphor - Designated on September 17, 1960. === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Shizuoka City|Q174691|WD=Q174691|1=Shizuoka (city)}}{{ill|lt=Shimizu Ward|Q79385|WD=Q79385|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}}{{ill|lt=Kusanagi (Shizuoka City)|Q11618222|WD=Q11618222|lt=Kusanagi|1=Kusanagi}}349 '''Access''' * From {{ill|lt=Tokai Passenger Railway|Q513679|WD=Q513679|1=Central Japan Railway Company}} (JR Tokai) {{ill|lt=Tokaido Line (Shizuoka Area)|Q11527981|WD=Q11527981|lt=Tokaido Main Line|1=Tōkaidō Line (Shizuoka area)}} {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Station (JR Tokai)|Q871079|WD=Q871079|lt=Kusanagi Station|1=Kusanagi Station (JR Central)}} ** Bus: Take the Shizutetsu Just Line (Miho Kusanagi Line) and get off at the "Kusanagi Jinja-mae" bus stop (about 7 minutes on the bus, a short walk after getting off) '''Surroundings''' * Hyōtanzuka Tumulus (Nishinohara No. 1 Tumulus, {{Wiki coordinates|34|59|53.74|N|138|26|40.83|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|location|name=Hyotanzuka Tumulus}}) - A keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of approximately 45.8 meters {{Sfn|Nishinohara Tumulus Group (Taira)|1979}}. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="Note"}} '''Source''' {{reflist|2}} === References === * Shrine history book * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Meiji Shrine Library|author=|year=1912|chapter=Kusanagi Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Meiji Shrine Library|Q11486103|WD=Q11486103|1=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Library|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine Library|1912}}}} ** [{{NDLDC|1088244/883}} "Meiji Shrine Journal Materials Fuken Gosha Volume 1"] (National Diet Library Digital Collection) Reference frames 883-884. * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Kenichi Tanikawa|editor-link=Kenichi Tanikawa|author=Kanichi Nomoto|authorlink=Kanichi Nomoto|year=1987|chapter=Kusanagi Shrine|title=Japanese Gods -Shrines and Sacred Sites- 10 Tokai|publisher={{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}|isbn=4560022208|ref={{Harvid|Kusanagi Shrine (Gods)|1987}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title=Japanese Historical Place Name System 22 Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490220|ref=}}** {{Wikicite|reference="Kusanagi Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Kusanagi Shrine (Taira)|2000}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Nishinohara Tumulus Group"|ref={{Harvid|Nishinohara Tumulus Group (Taira)|1979}}}} ===External link=== [https://kusanagijinjya.jp/ Kusanagi Shrine official website] === Related items === {{Commonscat|Kusanagi-jinja (Kusanagi, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka)}} * {{ill|lt=Amamuragumo Ken|Q14944|WD=Q14944|1=Kusanagi no Tsurugi}} (Kusanagi Ken) * {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Shrine|Q11368944|WD=Q11368944|1=Kusanagi Shrine (Q11368944)}} * {{ill|lt=Yaizu Shrine|Q3571342|WD=Q3571342|1=Yaizu Shrine}} * {{ill|lt=Katori Shrine|Q372380|WD=Q372380|1=Katori Jingū}} - {{ill|lt=SMAP|Q1138250|WD=Q1138250|1=SMAP}} A shrine with the same name as the member. {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kusanaki Shinsiya}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kusanagi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kusanagi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Kusanagi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suruga Province]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:清水区の歴史]] [[Category:駿河国の式内社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧県社]] [[Category:ヤマトタケル伝説]] [[Category:清水区の建築物]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]]
